<style>

/* Main banner container. Banner: background gradient, border colour and width and adding a margin between banner and article header*/
/* Important -- background gradient -- If you change one you must change all*/
.cnotice {
    position: relative;
    overflow: hidden;
    margin-bottom: 1em;
    cursor: pointer;
    background-color: #f7cac9;
    font-weight: 500;
    margin-bottom: 10px;
}

/* Sets the minimum banner height. If img + logo-container margins > height. Banner will be larger*/
#cnotice-main {
    display: table;
    width: 100%;
    height: 86px; /* need to set height for height 100% to work on elements within it */
}

.cnotice-message-container,
.cnotice-logo-container {
    display: table-cell;
    height: 100%;
}

/* --- Main message --- */
.cnotice-message-container {
    vertical-align: middle;
}

.cnotice-message {
    position: relative;
    margin: 0;
    color: #000;
    line-height: 1.2;
    padding: 11px 10px 11px 10px;
}

@media (min-width: 1200px) {
    .cnotice-message {
         padding: 11px 180px 11px 10px;
    }
}

body.rtl .cnotice-message {
    padding: 11px 40px 11px 140px;
    background-position: right 10px top 10px;
}

.cnotice-message p {
    margin: 0;
    font-family: "Linux Libertine", Georgia, Times, serif;
    font-size: 1.5em;
    line-height: 1.4;
    opacity: 1;
    text-align: center;
    padding-top: 10px;
    padding-bottom: 10px;
}

@media (min-width: 1200px) {
    .cnotice-message p { font-size: 2.0em; }
}


/* --- Logo Image text --- */

.cnotice-logo-container {
    width: 103px;
    vertical-align: top;
}

@media (min-width: 1200px) {
     .cnotice-logo-container { width: 140px; }
}

body.rtl .cnotice-logo-container {
}

#cnotice-logo-img {
    background-image: url(https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/donate/9/92/MokaHands_CN_local.png);
    background-repeat: no-repeat;
    background-size: contain;
    opacity: 0.6;
    width: 100%;
    height: 100%;
}

/* --- Close Options --- */

#cnotice-toggle-box-options {
    display: inline-block;
    position: absolute;
    right: .5em;
    top: .5em;
    font-size: .8em;
    text-transform: uppercase;
}

body.rtl #cnotice-toggle-box-options {
    right: auto;
    left: .5em;
}

#cnotice-toggle-box {
    display: inline-block;
    cursor: pointer;
    opacity: .5;
}

#cnotice-toggle-box:hover { opacity: 1; }

#cnotice-toggle-box-x {
    background: url('//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/donate/3/32/Close-thin.png') no-repeat center center;
    width: 9px;
    height: 9px;
    float: right;
    display: block;
    margin: .2em .4em;
}

body.rtl #cnotice-toggle-box-x {
    float: left;
}

/* --- Full Banner Link --- */
.cnotice a.cnotice-full-banner-click {
    display: block;
    height: 100%;
    width: 100%;
    cursor: pointer;
    text-decoration: none;
}

.cnotice a.cnotice-full-banner-click:hover {
    text-decoration: underline
}

.cnotice-button {
    border: none;
    display: none;
    border-radius: 4px;
    color: white;
    padding: 10px 15px;
    text-align: center;
    font-weight: bold;
    text-decoration: none;
    font-size: 17px;
    margin: 4px 2px;
    -webkit-transition-duration: 0.4s; /* Safari */
    transition-duration: 0.4s;
    cursor: pointer;
    z-index: 999;
}

@media (min-width: 1200px) {
    .cnotice-button {
        display: inline-block;
    }
}
@media (max-width: 1200px) {
    .cnotice-button {
        width: 25%;
        margin: auto;
        margin-top: 10px;
    }
}

/* --- Buttons --- */
.cnotice a.cnotice-buttonlink {
    cursor: pointer;
    text-decoration: none;
    color: black;
}

.cnotice a.cnotice-buttonlink:hover {
    color: white;
}

.cnotice-button1 {
    background-color: #0063bf;
    color: white;
    position: static;
    top: 45%;
    right: 53px;
    transform: translateY(-45%);
    min-width: 110px;
}

.rtl .cnotice-button1 {
    left: 23px;
    right: auto;
}
@media (min-width: 1200px) { .cnotice-button1 { position: absolute; } }

.cnotice-button1:hover {
    background-color: #447FF5;
}

#cnotice-translation-link {
  position: absolute;
  right: 95px;
  top: 5px;
  font-size: 0.8em;
  white-space: nowrap;
}

#cnotice-translation-link:hover {
  text-decoration: underline;
}

.rtl #cnotice-translation-link {
  text-align: left;
  left: 95px;
}
</style>

<tr>
<th colspan="2" style="color:inherit; text-align: center; background-color: rgb(235,235,210)"><a href="/wiki/Synonym_(taxonomy)" title="Synonym (taxonomy)">Synonyms</a><sup id="cite_ref-MSW3_2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MSW3-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</th></tr>
<tr>
<td colspan="2" style="text-align: left">
<p>at least 48 published
</p>
</td></tr>

</tbody></table><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1289143401">@media(max-width:640px){body:not(.skin-minerva) .mw-parser-output .infobox{width:100%!important}body:not(.skin-minerva) .mw-parser-output .infobox th{width:50%}}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota tr{background:transparent!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota img{background:transparent}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota tr{background:transparent!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota img{background:white}}.mw-parser-output .infobox.biota .taxobox-edit-taxonomy img{background:transparent!important}body.skin-vector .mw-parser-output table.biota.infobox{margin-top:0.5em}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output table.biota.infobox tr.taxonrow2 td{padding:2px 10px}</style>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Points_of_a_horse.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Diagram of a horse with some parts labeled." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Points_of_a_horse.jpg/500px-Points_of_a_horse.jpg" decoding="async" width="500" height="328" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.5" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/85/Points_of_a_horse.jpg 2x" data-file-width="819" data-file-height="538"></a><figcaption>External anatomy of a horse<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>The <b>horse</b> (<i><b>Equus ferus caballus</b></i>)<sup id="cite_ref-MSW3_2-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MSW3-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> is a <a href="/wiki/Domestication" title="Domestication">domesticated</a>, <a href="/wiki/Odd-toed_ungulate" class="mw-redirect" title="Odd-toed ungulate">one-toed</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ungulate" title="Ungulate">hoofed mammal</a>. It belongs to the taxonomic family <a href="/wiki/Equidae" title="Equidae">Equidae</a> and is one of two <a href="/wiki/Extant_taxon" class="mw-redirect" title="Extant taxon">extant</a> <a href="/wiki/Subspecies" title="Subspecies">subspecies</a> of <a href="/wiki/Wild_horse" title="Wild horse"><i>Equus ferus</i></a>. The horse has <a href="/wiki/Evolution_of_the_horse" title="Evolution of the horse">evolved</a> over the past 45 to 55 million years from a small multi-toed creature, <i><a href="/wiki/Eohippus" title="Eohippus">Eohippus</a></i>, into the large single-toed animal of today, originally in North America. Humans began domesticating horses around 4000 <a href="/wiki/Common_Era" title="Common Era">BCE</a> in Central Asia, and their <a href="/wiki/Domestication_of_the_horse" title="Domestication of the horse">domestication</a> is believed to have been widespread by 3000 BCE. Horses in the subspecies <i>caballus</i> are domesticated, although some domesticated populations live in the wild as <a href="/wiki/Feral_horse" title="Feral horse">feral horses</a>. These feral populations are not true <a href="/wiki/Wild_horse" title="Wild horse">wild horses</a>, which are horses that have never been domesticated. There is an extensive, specialized vocabulary used to describe equine-related concepts, covering everything from <a href="/wiki/Equine_anatomy" title="Equine anatomy">anatomy</a> to life stages, size, <a href="/wiki/Equine_coat_color" title="Equine coat color">colors</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_markings" title="Horse markings">markings</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_breed" title="Horse breed">breeds</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait" title="Horse gait">locomotion</a>, and behavior.
</p><p>Horses are <a href="/wiki/Cursorial" title="Cursorial">adapted to run</a>, allowing them to quickly escape predators, and possess a good <a href="/wiki/Sense_of_balance" title="Sense of balance">sense of balance</a> and a strong <a href="/wiki/Fight-or-flight_response" title="Fight-or-flight response">fight-or-flight response</a>. Related to this ability to flee from predators in the wild is an unusual trait: horses are able to sleep both standing up and lying down, with younger horses tending to sleep significantly more than adults.<sup id="cite_ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Female horses, called <a href="/wiki/Mare" title="Mare">mares</a>, carry their young for approximately 11 months and a young horse, called a <a href="/wiki/Foal" title="Foal">foal</a>, can stand and run shortly following birth. Most domesticated horses begin training under a <a href="/wiki/Saddle" title="Saddle">saddle</a> or in a <a href="/wiki/Horse_harness" title="Horse harness">harness</a> between the ages of two and four. They reach full adult development by age five, and have an average lifespan of between 25 and 30 years.
</p><p>Horse breeds are loosely divided into three categories based on general temperament: spirited "hot bloods" with speed and endurance; "cold bloods", such as <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">draft horses</a> and some <a href="/wiki/Pony" title="Pony">ponies</a>, suitable for slow, heavy work; and "<a href="/wiki/Warmblood" title="Warmblood">warmbloods</a>", developed from crosses between hot bloods and cold bloods, often focusing on creating breeds for specific riding purposes, particularly in Europe. There are more than 300 breeds of horse in the world today, developed for many different uses.
</p><p>Horses and humans interact in a wide variety of sport competitions and non-competitive recreational pursuits as well as in working activities such as <a href="/wiki/Mounted_police" title="Mounted police">police work</a>, <a href="/wiki/Working_animal" title="Working animal">agriculture</a>, entertainment, and <a href="/wiki/Equine-assisted_therapy" title="Equine-assisted therapy">therapy</a>. Horses were historically used in warfare, from which a wide variety of <a href="/wiki/Equestrianism" title="Equestrianism">riding</a> and <a href="/wiki/Driving_(horse)" title="Driving (horse)">driving</a> techniques developed, using many different styles of <a href="/wiki/Horse_tack" title="Horse tack">equipment</a> and methods of control. Many products are derived from horses, including <a href="/wiki/Horse_meat" title="Horse meat">meat</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_milk" class="mw-redirect" title="Horse milk">milk</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horsehide" class="mw-redirect" title="Horsehide">hide</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horsehair" title="Horsehair">hair</a>, bone, and <a href="/wiki/Premarin" class="mw-redirect" title="Premarin">pharmaceuticals extracted from the urine of pregnant mares</a>. 
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Lifespan_and_life_stages">Lifespan and life stages</h2></div>
<p>Depending on breed, <a href="/wiki/Horse_care" class="mw-redirect" title="Horse care">management</a> and environment, the modern domestic horse has a life expectancy of 25 to 30 years.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger46_7-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger46-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Uncommonly, a few animals live into their 40s and, occasionally, beyond.<sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The oldest verifiable record was "<a href="/wiki/Old_Billy" title="Old Billy">Old Billy</a>", a 19th-century horse that lived to the age of 62.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger46_7-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger46-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In modern times, Sugar Puff, who had been listed in <i><a href="/wiki/Guinness_World_Records" title="Guinness World Records">Guinness World Records</a></i> as the world's oldest living pony, died in 2007 at age 56.<sup id="cite_ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Regardless of a horse or pony's actual birth date, for most competition purposes a year is added to its age each January 1 of each year in the Northern Hemisphere<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger46_7-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger46-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and each August 1 in the Southern Hemisphere.<sup id="cite_ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The exception is in <a href="/wiki/Endurance_riding" title="Endurance riding">endurance riding</a>, where the minimum age to compete is based on the animal's actual calendar age.<sup id="cite_ref-Endurance_12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Endurance-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The following terminology is used to describe horses of various ages:
</p>
<dl><dt><a href="/wiki/Foal" title="Foal">Foal</a></dt>
<dd>A horse of either sex less than one year old. A nursing foal is sometimes called a <i>suckling</i>, and a foal that has been weaned is called a <i>weanling</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger418_13-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger418-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Most domesticated foals are weaned at five to seven months of age, although foals can be weaned at four months with no adverse physical effects.<sup id="cite_ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Yearling_(horse)" title="Yearling (horse)">Yearling</a></dt>
<dd>A horse of either sex that is between one and two years old.<sup id="cite_ref-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Colt_(horse)" title="Colt (horse)">Colt</a></dt>
<dd>A male horse under the age of four.<sup id="cite_ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A common terminology error is to call any young horse a "colt", when the term actually only refers to young male horses.<sup id="cite_ref-17" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Filly" title="Filly">Filly</a></dt>
<dd>A female horse under the age of four.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger418_13-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger418-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Mare" title="Mare">Mare</a></dt>
<dd>A female horse four years old and older.<sup id="cite_ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Stallion" title="Stallion">Stallion</a></dt>
<dd>A non-castrated male horse four years old and older.<sup id="cite_ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The term "horse" is sometimes used colloquially to refer specifically to a stallion.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger420_20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger420-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd>
<dt><a href="/wiki/Gelding" title="Gelding">Gelding</a></dt>
<dd>A <a href="/wiki/Neutering" title="Neutering">castrated</a> male horse of any age.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger418_13-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger418-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></dd></dl>
<p>In <a href="/wiki/Horse_racing" title="Horse racing">horse racing</a>, these definitions may differ: For example, in the British Isles, <a href="/wiki/Thoroughbred" title="Thoroughbred">Thoroughbred</a> horse racing defines colts and fillies as less than five years old.<sup id="cite_ref-21" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, Australian Thoroughbred racing defines colts and fillies as less than four years old.<sup id="cite_ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Size_and_measurement">Size and measurement</h2></div>
<p>The height of horses is measured at the highest point of the <a href="/wiki/Withers" title="Withers">withers</a>, where the neck meets the <a href="/wiki/Back_(horse)" title="Back (horse)">back</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Whitaker77_23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Whitaker77-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This point is used because it is a stable point of the anatomy, unlike the head or neck, which move up and down in relation to the body of the horse.
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Horse-and-pony.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A large brown horse is chasing a small horse in a pasture." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Horse-and-pony.jpg/250px-Horse-and-pony.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Horse-and-pony.jpg/500px-Horse-and-pony.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2048" data-file-height="1365"></a><figcaption>Size varies greatly among horse breeds, as with this full-sized horse and small pony.</figcaption></figure>
<p>In English-speaking countries, the height of horses is often stated in units of <a href="/wiki/Hand_(unit)" title="Hand (unit)">hands</a> and inches: one hand is equal to 4 inches (101.6 mm). The height is expressed as the number of full hands, followed by a <a href="/wiki/Radix_point" class="mw-redirect" title="Radix point">point</a>, then the number of additional inches, and ending with the abbreviation "h" or "hh" (for "hands high"). Thus, a horse described as "15.2 h" is 15 hands plus 2 inches, for a total of 62 inches (157.5 cm) in height.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger51_24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger51-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The size of horses varies by breed, but also is influenced by <a href="/wiki/Equine_nutrition" title="Equine nutrition">nutrition</a>. Light-riding horses usually range in height from 14 to 16 hands (56 to 64 inches, 142 to 163 cm) and can weigh from 380 to 550 kilograms (840 to 1,210 lb).<sup id="cite_ref-25" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Larger-riding horses usually start at about 15.2 hands (62 inches, 157 cm) and often are as tall as 17 hands (68 inches, 173 cm), weighing from 500 to 600 kilograms (1,100 to 1,320 lb).<sup id="cite_ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Heavy or <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">draft horses</a> are usually at least 16 hands (64 inches, 163 cm) high and can be as tall as 18 hands (72 inches, 183 cm) high. They can weigh from about 700 to 1,000 kilograms (1,540 to 2,200 lb).<sup id="cite_ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The largest horse in recorded history was probably a <a href="/wiki/Shire_horse" title="Shire horse">Shire horse</a> named <a href="/wiki/Sampson_(horse)" title="Sampson (horse)">Mammoth</a>, who was born in 1848. He stood <span class="frac nowrap">21.2<sup><span style="position:absolute; left:-10000px"> </span>1</sup>⁄<sub>4</sub></span> hands (86.25 inches, 219 cm) high and his peak weight was estimated at 1,524 kilograms (3,360 lb).<sup id="cite_ref-Whitaker60_28-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Whitaker60-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The record holder for the smallest horse ever is <a href="/wiki/Thumbelina_(horse)" title="Thumbelina (horse)">Thumbelina</a>, a fully mature <a href="/wiki/Miniature_horse" title="Miniature horse">miniature horse</a> affected by <a href="/wiki/Dwarfism" title="Dwarfism">dwarfism</a>. She was 43 centimetres; 4.1 hands (17 in) tall and weighed 26 kg (57 lb).<sup id="cite_ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Ponies">Ponies</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Pony" title="Pony">Pony</a></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Pony" title="Pony">Ponies</a> are <a href="/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology)" title="Taxonomy (biology)">taxonomically</a> the same animals as horses. The distinction between a horse and pony is commonly drawn on the basis of height, especially for competition purposes. However, height alone is not dispositive; the difference between horses and ponies may also include aspects of <a href="/wiki/Phenotype" title="Phenotype">phenotype</a>, including conformation and temperament.
</p><p>The traditional standard for height of a horse or a pony at maturity is 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm). An animal 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm) or over is usually considered to be a horse and one less than 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm) a pony,<sup id="cite_ref-EnsmingerHT_31-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EnsmingerHT-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 12">: 12 </span></sup> but there are many exceptions to the traditional standard. In Australia, ponies are considered to be those under 14 hands (56 inches, 142 cm).<sup id="cite_ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For competition in the <a href="/wiki/Western_riding" title="Western riding">Western</a> division of the <a href="/wiki/United_States_Equestrian_Federation" title="United States Equestrian Federation">United States Equestrian Federation</a>, the cutoff is 14.1 hands (57 inches, 145 cm).<sup id="cite_ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/International_Federation_for_Equestrian_Sports" title="International Federation for Equestrian Sports">International Federation for Equestrian Sports</a>, the world governing body for horse sport, uses <a href="/wiki/Metric_system" title="Metric system">metric</a> measurements and defines a pony as being any horse measuring less than 148 centimetres (58.27 in) at the withers without shoes, which is just over 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm), and <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1154941027">.mw-parser-output .frac{white-space:nowrap}.mw-parser-output .frac .num,.mw-parser-output .frac .den{font-size:80%;line-height:0;vertical-align:super}.mw-parser-output .frac .den{vertical-align:sub}.mw-parser-output .sr-only{border:0;clip:rect(0,0,0,0);clip-path:polygon(0px 0px,0px 0px,0px 0px);height:1px;margin:-1px;overflow:hidden;padding:0;position:absolute;width:1px}</style>149 centimetres (58.66 in; 14.<span class="frac">2<span class="sr-only">+</span><span class="num">1</span>⁄<span class="den">2</span></span> hands), with shoes.<sup id="cite_ref-34" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-34"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Height is not the sole criterion for distinguishing horses from ponies. <a href="/wiki/Breed_registry" title="Breed registry">Breed registries</a> for horses that typically produce individuals both under and over 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm) consider all animals of that breed to be horses regardless of their height.<sup id="cite_ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Conversely, some pony breeds may have features in common with horses, and individual animals may occasionally mature at over 14.2 hands (58 inches, 147 cm), but are still considered to be ponies.<sup id="cite_ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Ponies often exhibit thicker manes, tails, and coats. They also have proportionally shorter legs, wider barrels, heavier <dfn><a href="/wiki/Glossary_of_equestrian_terms#bone" title="Glossary of equestrian terms"><span title="See entry at: Glossary of equestrian terms § bone" style="color:inherit;" class="glossary-link">bone</span></a></dfn>, shorter and thicker necks, and short heads with broad foreheads. They may have calmer temperaments than horses and also a high level of intelligence that may or may not be used to cooperate with human handlers.<sup id="cite_ref-EnsmingerHT_31-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EnsmingerHT-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Pages: 11–12">: 11–12 </span></sup><sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability" title="Wikipedia:Verifiability"><span title="Is not found on pages 11-12, and doesn't appear to be in this book. (April 2024)">failed verification</span></a></i>]</sup> Small size, by itself, is not an exclusive determinant. For example, the <a href="/wiki/Shetland_pony" title="Shetland pony">Shetland pony</a> which averages 10 hands (40 inches, 102 cm), is considered a pony.<sup id="cite_ref-EnsmingerHT_31-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EnsmingerHT-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 12">: 12 </span></sup>Conversely, breeds such as the <a href="/wiki/Falabella" title="Falabella">Falabella</a> and other <a href="/wiki/Miniature_horse" title="Miniature horse">miniature horses</a>, which can be no taller than 76 centimetres; 7.2 hands (30 in), are classified by their <a href="/wiki/Breed_registry" title="Breed registry">registries</a> as very small horses, not ponies.<sup id="cite_ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Genetics">Genetics</h2></div>
<p>Horses have 64 <a href="/wiki/Chromosome" title="Chromosome">chromosomes</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Horse_genome" title="Horse genome">horse genome</a> was <a href="/wiki/DNA_sequencing" title="DNA sequencing">sequenced</a> in 2007. It contains 2.7 billion DNA <a href="/wiki/Base_pair" title="Base pair">base pairs</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Cornell_39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cornell-39"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which is larger than the <a href="/wiki/Dog_genome" class="mw-redirect" title="Dog genome">dog genome</a>, but smaller than the <a href="/wiki/Human_genome" title="Human genome">human genome</a> or the <a href="/wiki/Bovine_genome" title="Bovine genome">bovine genome</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-ScienceDaily_40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ScienceDaily-40"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The map is available to researchers.<sup id="cite_ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Colors_and_markings">Colors and markings</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Equine_coat_color" title="Equine coat color">Equine coat color</a>, <a href="/wiki/Equine_coat_color_genetics" title="Equine coat color genetics">Equine coat color genetics</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Horse_markings" title="Horse markings">Horse markings</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Horsescd1l-095.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Two horses in a field. The one on the left is a dark brown with a black mane and tail. The one on the right is a light red all over." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Horsescd1l-095.jpg/500px-Horsescd1l-095.jpg" decoding="async" width="500" height="328" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.4" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/24/Horsescd1l-095.jpg 2x" data-file-width="524" data-file-height="344"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Bay_(horse)" title="Bay (horse)">Bay</a> (left) and <a href="/wiki/Chestnut_(horse_color)" title="Chestnut (horse color)">chestnut</a> (right) are two of the most common coat colors, seen in almost all breeds.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses exhibit a diverse array of <a href="/wiki/Equine_coat_color" title="Equine coat color">coat colors</a> and distinctive <a href="/wiki/Horse_markings" title="Horse markings">markings</a>, described by a specialized vocabulary. Often, a horse is classified first by its coat color, before breed or sex.<sup id="cite_ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses of the same color may be distinguished from one another by white <a href="/wiki/Horse_markings" title="Horse markings">markings</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which, along with various spotting patterns, are inherited separately from coat color.<sup id="cite_ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many <a href="/wiki/Equine_coat_color_genetics" title="Equine coat color genetics">genes</a> that create horse coat colors and patterns have been identified. Current genetic tests can identify at least 13 different <a href="/wiki/Allele" title="Allele">alleles</a> influencing coat color,<sup id="cite_ref-UCVGL_45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UCVGL-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and research continues to discover new genes linked to specific traits. The basic coat colors of <a href="/wiki/Chestnut_(horse_color)" title="Chestnut (horse color)">chestnut</a> and <a href="/wiki/Black_horse" title="Black horse">black</a> are determined by the <a href="/wiki/Gene" title="Gene">gene</a> controlled by the <a href="/wiki/Melanocortin_1_receptor" title="Melanocortin 1 receptor">Melanocortin 1 receptor</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Marklund1996_46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Marklund1996-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> also known as the "extension gene" or "red factor".<sup id="cite_ref-UCVGL_45-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UCVGL-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Its recessive form is "red" (chestnut) and its dominant form is black.<sup id="cite_ref-UCDIntro_47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UCDIntro-47"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Additional <a href="/wiki/Gene" title="Gene">genes</a> control suppression of black color to <a href="/wiki/Point_coloration" class="mw-redirect" title="Point coloration">point coloration</a> that results in a <a href="/wiki/Bay_(horse)" title="Bay (horse)">bay</a>, spotting patterns such as <a href="/wiki/Pinto_horse" title="Pinto horse">pinto</a> or <a href="/wiki/Leopard_complex" title="Leopard complex">leopard</a>, <a href="/wiki/Dilution_gene" title="Dilution gene">dilution genes</a> such as <a href="/wiki/Palomino" title="Palomino">palomino</a> or <a href="/wiki/Dun_gene" title="Dun gene">dun</a>, as well as <a href="/wiki/Gray_horse" title="Gray horse">greying</a>, and all the other factors that create the many possible coat colors found in horses.<sup id="cite_ref-UCVGL_45-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UCVGL-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses that have a white coat color are often mislabeled; a horse that looks "white" is usually a middle-aged or older <a href="/wiki/Gray_horse" title="Gray horse">gray</a>. Grays are born a darker shade, get lighter as they age, but usually keep black skin underneath their white hair coat (with the exception of pink skin under white <a href="/wiki/Horse_markings" title="Horse markings">markings</a>). The only horses properly called <a href="/wiki/White_horse" title="White horse">white</a> are born with a predominantly white hair coat and pink skin, a fairly rare occurrence.<sup id="cite_ref-UCDIntro_47-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UCDIntro-47"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Different and unrelated <a href="/wiki/Genetics" title="Genetics">genetic</a> factors can produce white coat colors in horses, including several different alleles of <a href="/wiki/Dominant_white" title="Dominant white">dominant white</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Sabino_horse" title="Sabino horse">sabino-1 gene</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-haase2009-similar_48-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-haase2009-similar-48"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, there are no "<a href="/wiki/Albinism" title="Albinism">albino</a>" horses, defined as having both pink skin and red eyes.<sup id="cite_ref-Duplicatetest_49-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Duplicatetest-49"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Reproduction_and_development">Reproduction and development</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Horse_breeding" title="Horse breeding">Horse breeding</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mare_foal_poland.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Mare_foal_poland.jpg/250px-Mare_foal_poland.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Mare_foal_poland.jpg/500px-Mare_foal_poland.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4608" data-file-height="3456"></a><figcaption>A mare with a foal</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Gestation" title="Gestation">Gestation</a> lasts approximately 340 days, with an average range 320–370 days,<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger156_50-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger156-50"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and usually results in one <a href="/wiki/Foal" title="Foal">foal</a>; twins are rare.<sup id="cite_ref-51" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-51"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses are a precocial species, and foals are capable of standing and running within a short time following birth.<sup id="cite_ref-52" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-52"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Foals are usually born in the spring. The <a href="/wiki/Estrous_cycle" title="Estrous cycle">estrous cycle</a> of a mare occurs roughly every 19–22 days and occurs from early spring into autumn. Most mares enter an <i>anestrus</i> period during the winter and thus do not cycle in this period.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger150_53-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger150-53"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Foals are generally <a href="/wiki/Weaning" title="Weaning">weaned</a> from their mothers between four and six months of age.<sup id="cite_ref-54" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-54"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses, particularly colts, are sometimes physically capable of reproduction at about 18 months, but domesticated horses are rarely allowed to breed before the age of three, especially females.<sup id="cite_ref-EnsmingerHT_31-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EnsmingerHT-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 129">: 129 </span></sup> Horses four years old are considered mature, although the skeleton normally continues to develop until the age of six; maturation also depends on the horse's size, breed, sex, and quality of care. Larger horses have larger bones; therefore, not only do the bones take longer to form <a href="/wiki/Bone" title="Bone">bone tissue</a>, but the <a href="/wiki/Epiphyseal_plate" title="Epiphyseal plate">epiphyseal plates</a> are larger and take longer to convert from <a href="/wiki/Cartilage" title="Cartilage">cartilage</a> to bone. These plates convert after the other parts of the bones, and are crucial to development.<sup id="cite_ref-55" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-55"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Depending on maturity, breed, and work expected, horses are usually put under saddle and <a href="/wiki/Horse_training" title="Horse training">trained</a> to be ridden between the ages of two and four.<sup id="cite_ref-Train163_56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Train163-56"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Although <a href="/wiki/Thoroughbred" title="Thoroughbred">Thoroughbred</a> <a href="/wiki/Horse_racing" title="Horse racing">race horses</a> are put on the track as young as the age of two in some countries,<sup id="cite_ref-57" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-57"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> horses specifically bred for sports such as <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a> are generally not put under saddle until they are three or four years old, because their bones and muscles are not solidly developed.<sup id="cite_ref-58" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-58"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For <a href="/wiki/Endurance_riding" title="Endurance riding">endurance riding</a> competition, horses are not deemed mature enough to compete until they are a full 60 calendar months (five years) old.<sup id="cite_ref-Endurance_12-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Endurance-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Anatomy">Anatomy</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Equine_anatomy" title="Equine anatomy">Equine anatomy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Muscular_system_of_the_horse" title="Muscular system of the horse">Muscular system of the horse</a>, <a href="/wiki/Respiratory_system_of_the_horse" title="Respiratory system of the horse">Respiratory system of the horse</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Circulatory_system_of_the_horse" title="Circulatory system of the horse">Circulatory system of the horse</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Skeletal_system">Skeletal system</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Skeletal_system_of_the_horse" title="Skeletal system of the horse">Skeletal system of the horse</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Horse_anatomy.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Diagram of a horse skeleton with major parts labeled." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Horse_anatomy.svg/500px-Horse_anatomy.svg.png" decoding="async" width="500" height="393" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.4" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Horse_anatomy.svg/960px-Horse_anatomy.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="512" data-file-height="402"></a><figcaption>The skeletal system of a modern horse</figcaption></figure>
<p>The horse skeleton averages 205 bones.<sup id="cite_ref-Evans90_59-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Evans90-59"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A significant difference between the horse skeleton and that of a human is the lack of a <a href="/wiki/Clavicle" title="Clavicle">collarbone</a>—the horse's <a href="/wiki/Equine_forelimb_anatomy" class="mw-redirect" title="Equine forelimb anatomy">forelimbs</a> are attached to the <a href="/wiki/Vertebral_column" class="mw-redirect" title="Vertebral column">spinal column</a> by a powerful set of muscles, tendons, and ligaments that attach the <a href="/wiki/Scapula" title="Scapula">shoulder blade</a> to the torso. The horse's four legs and hooves are also unique structures. Their leg bones are proportioned differently from those of a human. For example, the body part that is called a horse's "knee" is actually made up of the <a href="/wiki/Carpal_bones" title="Carpal bones">carpal bones</a> that correspond to the human <a href="/wiki/Wrist" title="Wrist">wrist</a>. Similarly, the <a href="/wiki/Hock_(anatomy)" title="Hock (anatomy)">hock</a> contains bones equivalent to those in the human <a href="/wiki/Ankle" title="Ankle">ankle</a> and <a href="/wiki/Heel" title="Heel">heel</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger21_60-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger21-60"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The lower leg bones of a horse correspond to the bones of the human hand or foot. The <a href="/wiki/Equine_anatomy" title="Equine anatomy">fetlock</a>, incorrectly called the "ankle", is actually the proximal <a href="/wiki/Sesamoid_bone" title="Sesamoid bone">sesamoid bones</a> between the <a href="/wiki/Equine_anatomy" title="Equine anatomy">cannon</a> bones (a single equivalent to the human <a href="/wiki/Metacarpus" class="mw-redirect" title="Metacarpus">metacarpal</a> or <a href="/wiki/Metatarsal_bones" title="Metatarsal bones">metatarsal bones</a>) and the <a href="/wiki/Proximal_phalanges" class="mw-redirect" title="Proximal phalanges">proximal phalanges</a>, located where one finds the "knuckles" of a human. A horse has no muscles in its legs below the knees and hocks, only skin, hair, bone, <a href="/wiki/Tendon" title="Tendon">tendons</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ligament" title="Ligament">ligaments</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cartilage" title="Cartilage">cartilage</a>, and the assorted specialized tissues that make up the <a href="/wiki/Horse_hoof" title="Horse hoof">hoof</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger21_60-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger21-60"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Hooves">Hooves</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Horse_hoof" title="Horse hoof">Horse hoof</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horseshoe" title="Horseshoe">Horseshoe</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Farrier" title="Farrier">Farrier</a></div>
<p>The critical importance of the feet and legs is summed up by the traditional adage, "no foot, no horse".<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger367_61-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger367-61"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Horse_hoof" title="Horse hoof">horse hoof</a> begins with the <a href="/wiki/Distal_phalanges" class="mw-redirect" title="Distal phalanges">distal phalanges</a>, the equivalent of the human fingertip or tip of the toe, surrounded by <a href="/wiki/Cartilage" title="Cartilage">cartilage</a> and other specialized, blood-rich soft tissues such as the <a href="/wiki/Horse_hoof#Internal_structures" title="Horse hoof">laminae</a>. The exterior hoof wall and horn of the sole is made of <a href="/wiki/Keratin" title="Keratin">keratin</a>, the same material as a human <a href="/wiki/Nail_(anatomy)" title="Nail (anatomy)">fingernail</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-62" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-62"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>The result is that a horse, weighing on average 500 kilograms (1,100 lb),<sup id="cite_ref-63" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-63"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> travels on the same bones as would a human on tiptoe.<sup id="cite_ref-64" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-64"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For the protection of the hoof under certain conditions, some horses have <a href="/wiki/Horseshoe" title="Horseshoe">horseshoes</a> placed on their feet by a professional <a href="/wiki/Farrier" title="Farrier">farrier</a>. The hoof continually grows, and in most domesticated horses needs to be trimmed (and <a href="/wiki/Horseshoe" title="Horseshoe">horseshoes</a> reset, if used) every five to eight weeks,<sup id="cite_ref-65" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-65"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> though the hooves of horses in the wild wear down and regrow at a rate suitable for their terrain.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Teeth">Teeth</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Horse_teeth" title="Horse teeth">Horse teeth</a></div>
<p>Horses are adapted to <a href="/wiki/Grazing" title="Grazing">grazing</a>. In an adult horse, there are 12 <a href="/wiki/Incisor" title="Incisor">incisors</a> at the front of the mouth, adapted to biting off the grass or other vegetation. There are 24 teeth adapted for chewing, the <a href="/wiki/Premolar" title="Premolar">premolars</a> and <a href="/wiki/Molar_(tooth)" title="Molar (tooth)">molars</a>, at the back of the mouth. Stallions and geldings have four additional teeth just behind the incisors, a type of <a href="/wiki/Canine_tooth" title="Canine tooth">canine teeth</a> called "tushes". Some horses, both male and female, will also develop one to four very small <a href="/wiki/Vestigiality" title="Vestigiality">vestigial</a> teeth in front of the molars, known as "wolf" teeth, which are generally removed because they can interfere with the <a href="/wiki/Bit_(horse)" title="Bit (horse)">bit</a>. There is an empty interdental space between the incisors and the molars where the bit rests directly on the gums, or "bars" of the horse's mouth when the horse is <a href="/wiki/Bridle" title="Bridle">bridled</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-66" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-66"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>An estimate of a horse's age can be made from looking at its teeth. The teeth continue to erupt throughout life and are worn down by grazing. Therefore, the incisors show changes as the horse ages; they develop a distinct wear pattern, changes in tooth shape, and changes in the angle at which the chewing surfaces meet. This allows a very rough estimate of a horse's age, although diet and veterinary care can also affect the rate of tooth wear.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger46_7-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger46-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Digestion">Digestion</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Equine_anatomy#Digestive_system" title="Equine anatomy">Equine digestive system</a> and <a href="/wiki/Equine_nutrition" title="Equine nutrition">Equine nutrition</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%A2%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%B3%D1%8B%D1%80%D0%B5_(%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/%D0%9B%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%A2%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%B3%D1%8B%D1%80%D0%B5_%28%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0%29.jpg/250px-%D0%9B%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%A2%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%B3%D1%8B%D1%80%D0%B5_%28%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/%D0%9B%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%A2%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%B3%D1%8B%D1%80%D0%B5_%28%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0%29.jpg/500px-%D0%9B%D0%BE%D1%88%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%A2%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%B9%D0%B3%D1%8B%D1%80%D0%B5_%28%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%91%D0%B0%D1%8F%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%83%D0%BB%D0%B0%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="6048" data-file-height="4032"></a><figcaption>A horse drinking in <a href="/wiki/Bayanaul_National_Park" title="Bayanaul National Park">Bayanaul National Park</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kazakhstan" title="Kazakhstan">Kazakhstan</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses are <a href="/wiki/Herbivore" title="Herbivore">herbivores</a> with a digestive system adapted to a <a href="/wiki/Forage" title="Forage">forage</a> diet of grasses and other plant material, consumed steadily throughout the day. Therefore, compared to humans, they have a relatively small stomach but very long intestines to facilitate a steady flow of nutrients. A 450-kilogram (990 lb) horse will eat 7 to 11 kilograms (15 to 24 lb) of food per day and, under normal use, drink 38 to 45 litres (8.4 to 9.9 imp gal; 10 to 12 US gal) of <a href="/wiki/Water" title="Water">water</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-67" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-67"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses are not <a href="/wiki/Ruminant" title="Ruminant">ruminants</a>, having only one stomach, like humans. But unlike humans, they can digest <a href="/wiki/Cellulose" title="Cellulose">cellulose</a>, a major component of grass, through the process of  <a href="/wiki/Hindgut_fermentation" title="Hindgut fermentation">hindgut fermentation</a>. Cellulose fermentation by symbiotic bacteria and other microbes occurs in the <a href="/wiki/Cecum" title="Cecum">cecum</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Large_intestine" title="Large intestine">large intestine</a>. Horses cannot <a href="/wiki/Vomiting" title="Vomiting">vomit</a>, so digestion problems can quickly cause <a href="/wiki/Horse_colic" title="Horse colic">colic</a>, a leading cause of death.<sup id="cite_ref-68" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-68"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Although horses do not have a <a href="/wiki/Gallbladder" title="Gallbladder">gallbladder</a>, they tolerate high amounts of fat in their diet.<sup id="cite_ref-69" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-69"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-70" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-70"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Senses">Senses</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Equine_vision" title="Equine vision">Equine vision</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Pferdeauge.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Close up of a horse eye, which is dark brown with lashes on the top eyelid" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Pferdeauge.jpg/250px-Pferdeauge.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Pferdeauge.jpg/500px-Pferdeauge.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1352" data-file-height="901"></a><figcaption>A horse's eye</figcaption></figure>
<p>The horses' senses are based on their status as <a href="/wiki/Predation" title="Predation">prey animals</a>, where they must be aware of their surroundings at all times.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger309_71-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger309-71"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The equine eye is one of the largest of any land mammal.<sup id="cite_ref-Hartley_2016_72-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hartley_2016-72"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses are lateral-eyed, meaning that their eyes are positioned on the sides of their heads.<sup id="cite_ref-73" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-73"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This means that horses have a range of vision of more than 350°, with approximately 65° of this being <a href="/wiki/Binocular_vision" title="Binocular vision">binocular vision</a> and the remaining 285° <a href="/wiki/Monocular_vision" title="Monocular vision">monocular vision</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Sellnow_74-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sellnow-74"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses have excellent day and <a href="/wiki/Night_vision" title="Night vision">night vision</a>, but they have two-color, or <a href="/wiki/Dichromacy" title="Dichromacy">dichromatic vision</a>; their <a href="/wiki/Color_vision" title="Color vision">color vision</a> is somewhat like red-green <a href="/wiki/Color_blindness" title="Color blindness">color blindness</a> in humans, where certain colors, especially red and related colors, appear as a shade of green.<sup id="cite_ref-75" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-75"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Their <a href="/wiki/Olfaction" class="mw-redirect" title="Olfaction">sense of smell</a>, while much better than that of humans, is not quite as good as that of a dog. It is believed to play a key role in the social interactions of horses as well as detecting other key scents in the environment. Horses have two olfactory centers. The first system is in the nostrils and nasal cavity, which analyze a wide range of odors. The second, located under the nasal cavity, is the <a href="/wiki/Vomeronasal_organ" title="Vomeronasal organ">vomeronasal organs</a>, also called Jacobson's organs. These have a separate nerve pathway to the brain and appear to primarily analyze <a href="/wiki/Pheromone" title="Pheromone">pheromones</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Briggs_smell_76-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Briggs_smell-76"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A horse's hearing is good,<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger309_71-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger309-71"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Auricle_(anatomy)" title="Auricle (anatomy)">pinna</a> of each ear can rotate up to 180°, giving the potential for 360° hearing without having to move the head.<sup id="cite_ref-77" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-77"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Noise affects the behavior of horses and certain kinds of noise may contribute to stress—a 2013 study in the UK indicated that stabled horses were calmest in a quiet setting, or if listening to country or classical music, but displayed signs of nervousness when listening to jazz or rock music. This study also recommended keeping music under a volume of 21 <a href="/wiki/Decibel" title="Decibel">decibels</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Music_78-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Music-78"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> An Australian study found that stabled racehorses listening to talk radio had a higher rate of gastric ulcers than horses listening to music, and racehorses stabled where a radio was played had a higher overall rate of ulceration than horses stabled where there was no radio playing.<sup id="cite_ref-EquiNews_79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EquiNews-79"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses have a great sense of balance, due partly to their ability to feel their footing and partly to highly developed <a href="/wiki/Proprioception" title="Proprioception">proprioception</a>—the unconscious sense of where the body and limbs are at all times.<sup id="cite_ref-80" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-80"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A horse's <a href="/wiki/Somatosensory_system" title="Somatosensory system">sense of touch</a> is well-developed. The most sensitive areas are around the eyes, ears, and nose.<sup id="cite_ref-81" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-81"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses are able to sense contact as subtle as an insect landing anywhere on the body.<sup id="cite_ref-82" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-82"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses have an advanced sense of taste, which allows them to sort through <a href="/wiki/Fodder" title="Fodder">fodder</a> and choose what they would most like to eat,<sup id="cite_ref-83" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-83"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and their <a href="/wiki/Prehensility" title="Prehensility">prehensile</a> lips can easily sort even small grains. Horses generally will not eat poisonous plants; however, there are exceptions. Horses will occasionally eat toxic amounts of poisonous plants even when there is adequate healthy food.<sup id="cite_ref-84" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-84"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Movement">Movement</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait" title="Horse gait">Horse gait</a>, <a href="/wiki/Trot" title="Trot">Trot</a>, <a href="/wiki/Canter" class="mw-redirect" title="Canter">Canter</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Ambling" class="mw-redirect" title="Ambling">Ambling</a></div>
<p>All horses move naturally with four basic <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait" title="Horse gait">gaits</a>:<sup id="cite_ref-HorseGaits32_85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HorseGaits32-85"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul><li>the four-beat <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait#Walk" title="Horse gait">walk</a>, which averages 6.4 kilometres per hour (4.0 mph);</li>
<li>the two-beat <a href="/wiki/Trot" title="Trot">trot or jog</a> at 13 to 19 kilometres per hour (8.1 to 11.8 mph) (faster for <a href="/wiki/Harness_racing" title="Harness racing">harness racing</a> horses);</li>
<li>the <a href="/wiki/Canter_and_gallop" title="Canter and gallop">canter or lope</a>, a three-beat gait that is 19 to 24 kilometres per hour (12 to 15 mph);</li>
<li>the <a href="/wiki/Canter_and_gallop" title="Canter and gallop">gallop</a>, which averages 40 to 48 kilometres per hour (25 to 30 mph),<sup id="cite_ref-Harris47_86-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Harris47-86"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> but the world record for a horse galloping over a short, sprint distance is 70.76 kilometres per hour (43.97 mph).<sup id="cite_ref-guinness_87-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-guinness-87"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<p>Besides these basic gaits, some horses perform a two-beat <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait" title="Horse gait">pace</a>, instead of the trot.<sup id="cite_ref-HorseGaits50_88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HorseGaits50-88"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There also are several four-beat '<a href="/wiki/Ambling" class="mw-redirect" title="Ambling">ambling</a>' gaits that are approximately the speed of a trot or pace, though smoother to ride. These include the lateral <a href="/wiki/Ambling#Rack" class="mw-redirect" title="Ambling">rack</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ambling" class="mw-redirect" title="Ambling">running walk</a>, and <a href="/wiki/T%C3%B6lt" class="mw-redirect" title="Tölt">tölt</a> as well as the diagonal <a href="/wiki/Ambling_gait" title="Ambling gait">fox trot</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Lieberman_89-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lieberman-89"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Ambling gaits are often genetic in some breeds, known collectively as <a href="/wiki/Gaited_horse" class="mw-redirect" title="Gaited horse">gaited horses</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-90" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-90"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> These horses replace the trot with one of the ambling gaits.<sup id="cite_ref-HorseGaits51_91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HorseGaits51-91"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
