<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Behavior">Behavior</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Horse_behavior" title="Horse behavior">Horse behavior</a> and <a href="/wiki/Stable_vices" title="Stable vices">Stable vices</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><span class="mw-tmh-player audio mw-file-element" style="width:250px;"><audio id="mwe_player_0_placeholder" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="" width="250" style="width:250px;" data-durationhint="3" data-mwtitle="Wiehern.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Wiehern.ogg" playsinline="" disabled="disabled" tabindex="-1"></audio><a class="mw-tmh-play" href="/wiki/File:Wiehern.ogg" title="Play audio" role="button"><span class="mw-tmh-play-icon notheme"></span></a><span class="mw-tmh-duration mw-tmh-label"><span class="sr-only">Duration: 3 seconds.</span><span aria-hidden="true">0:03</span></span></span></span><a href="/wiki/File:Wiehern.ogg" class="mw-file-magnify"></a><figcaption>Horse neigh</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses are prey animals with a strong <a href="/wiki/Fight-or-flight_response" title="Fight-or-flight response">fight-or-flight response</a>. Their first reaction to a threat is to startle and usually flee, although they will stand their ground and defend themselves when flight is impossible or if their young are threatened.<sup id="cite_ref-92" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-92"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They also tend to be curious; when startled, they will often hesitate an instant to ascertain the cause of their fright, and may not always flee from something that they perceive as non-threatening. Most light horse riding breeds were developed for speed, agility, alertness, and endurance, natural qualities that extend from their wild ancestors. However, through selective breeding, some breeds of horses are quite docile, particularly certain draft horses.<sup id="cite_ref-Natural226_93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Natural226-93"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Garranos_fight.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Garranos_fight.jpg/250px-Garranos_fight.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Garranos_fight.jpg/500px-Garranos_fight.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5706" data-file-height="3804"></a><figcaption>Horses fighting as part of herd dominance behaviour</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses are <a href="/wiki/Herd_animal" class="mw-redirect" title="Herd animal">herd animals</a>, with a clear hierarchy of rank, led by a dominant individual, usually a mare. They are also social creatures that are able to form companionship attachments to their own species and to other animals, including humans. They communicate in various ways, including vocalizations such as nickering or whinnying, mutual <a href="/wiki/Social_grooming" title="Social grooming">grooming</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Body_language" title="Body language">body language</a>. Many horses will become difficult to manage if they are isolated, but with training, horses can learn to accept a human as a companion, and thus be comfortable away from other horses.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger305_94-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger305-94"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, when confined with insufficient companionship, exercise, or stimulation, individuals may develop <a href="/wiki/Stable_vices" title="Stable vices">stable vices</a>, an assortment of bad habits, mostly <a href="/wiki/Stereotypy_(non-human)" title="Stereotypy (non-human)">stereotypies</a> of psychological origin, that include wood chewing, wall kicking, "weaving" (rocking back and forth), and other problems.<sup id="cite_ref-Prince214_95-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prince214-95"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Intelligence_and_learning">Intelligence and learning</h3></div>
<p>Studies have indicated that horses perform a number of <a href="/wiki/Cognition" title="Cognition">cognitive</a> tasks on a daily basis, meeting mental challenges that include <a href="/wiki/Foraging" title="Foraging">food procurement</a> and identification of individuals within a <a href="/wiki/Social_structure" title="Social structure">social system</a>. They also have good <a href="/wiki/Spatial_visualization_ability" class="mw-redirect" title="Spatial visualization ability">spatial discrimination</a> abilities.<sup id="cite_ref-Hanggi_96-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hanggi-96"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They are naturally curious and apt to investigate things they have not seen before.<sup id="cite_ref-97" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-97"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Studies have assessed equine intelligence in areas such as <a href="/wiki/Problem_solving" title="Problem solving">problem solving</a>, speed of learning, and <a href="/wiki/Memory" title="Memory">memory</a>. Horses excel at simple learning, but also are able to use more advanced cognitive abilities that involve <a href="/wiki/Categorization" class="mw-redirect" title="Categorization">categorization</a> and <a href="/wiki/Concept_learning" title="Concept learning">concept learning</a>. They can learn using <a href="/wiki/Habituation" title="Habituation">habituation</a>, <a href="/wiki/Desensitization_(psychology)" title="Desensitization (psychology)">desensitization</a>, <a href="/wiki/Classical_conditioning" title="Classical conditioning">classical conditioning</a>, <a href="/wiki/Operant_conditioning" title="Operant conditioning">operant conditioning</a>, and positive and negative <a href="/wiki/Reinforcement" title="Reinforcement">reinforcement</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Hanggi_96-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hanggi-96"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> One study has indicated that horses can differentiate between "more or less" if the quantity involved is less than four.<sup id="cite_ref-count_98-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-count-98"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Domesticated horses may face greater mental challenges than wild horses, because they live in artificial environments that prevent <a href="/wiki/Instinct" title="Instinct">instinctive</a> behavior whilst also learning tasks that are not natural.<sup id="cite_ref-Hanggi_96-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hanggi-96"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses are animals of <a href="/wiki/Habit" title="Habit">habit</a> that respond well to regimentation, and respond best when the same routines and techniques are used consistently. One trainer believes that "intelligent" horses are reflections of intelligent trainers who effectively use response conditioning techniques and positive reinforcement to train in the style that best fits with an individual animal's natural inclinations.<sup id="cite_ref-99" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-99"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>99<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Temperament">Temperament</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">Draft horse</a>, <a href="/wiki/Warmblood" title="Warmblood">Warmblood</a>, <a href="/wiki/Oriental_horse" title="Oriental horse">Oriental horse</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hot-blooded_horse" title="Hot-blooded horse">Hot-blooded horse</a></div>
<p>Horses are <a href="/wiki/Mammal" title="Mammal">mammals</a>. As such, they are <a href="/wiki/Warm-blooded" title="Warm-blooded">warm-blooded</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Endotherm" title="Endotherm">endothermic</a> creatures, as opposed to cold-blooded, or <a href="/wiki/Poikilotherm" title="Poikilotherm">poikilothermic</a> animals. However, these words have developed a separate meaning in the context of equine terminology, used to describe temperament, not <a href="/wiki/Thermoregulation" title="Thermoregulation">body temperature</a>. For example, the "<a href="/wiki/Hot-blooded_horse" title="Hot-blooded horse">hot-bloods</a>", such as many <a href="/wiki/Horse_racing" title="Horse racing">race horses</a>, exhibit more sensitivity and energy,<sup id="cite_ref-Belknap255_100-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Belknap255-100"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> while the "cold-bloods", such as most <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">draft breeds</a>, are quieter and calmer.<sup id="cite_ref-Belknap112_101-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Belknap112-101"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sometimes "hot-bloods" are classified as "light horses" or "riding horses",<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger71_102-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger71-102"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> with the "cold-bloods" classified as "draft horses" or "work horses".<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger84_103-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger84-103"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>"Hot blooded" breeds include "<a href="/wiki/Oriental_horse" title="Oriental horse">oriental horses</a>" such as the <a href="/wiki/Akhal-Teke" title="Akhal-Teke">Akhal-Teke</a>, <a href="/wiki/Arabian_horse" title="Arabian horse">Arabian horse</a>, <a href="/wiki/Barb_horse" title="Barb horse">Barb</a>, and now-extinct <a href="/wiki/Turkoman_horse" title="Turkoman horse">Turkoman horse</a>, as well as the <a href="/wiki/Thoroughbred" title="Thoroughbred">Thoroughbred</a>, a breed developed in England from the older oriental breeds.<sup id="cite_ref-Belknap255_100-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Belknap255-100"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Hot bloods tend to be spirited, bold, and learn quickly. They are bred for agility and speed.<sup id="cite_ref-Catalog18_104-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Catalog18-104"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They tend to be physically refined—thin-skinned, slim, and long-legged.<sup id="cite_ref-105" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-105"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Over the centuries, European breeders imported oriental horses from the Middle East and Northern Africa for breeding when they wanted to incorporate characteristic traits into their best horse racing and light <a href="/wiki/Cavalry" title="Cavalry">cavalry</a> horses.<sup id="cite_ref-Whitaker43_106-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Whitaker43-106"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Whitaker194_107-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Whitaker194-107"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Muscular, heavy <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">draft horses</a> are known as "cold bloods", as they are bred not only for strength, but also to have the calm, patient temperament needed to pull a plow or a heavy carriage full of people.<sup id="cite_ref-Belknap112_101-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Belknap112-101"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They are sometimes nicknamed "gentle giants".<sup id="cite_ref-Catalog15_108-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Catalog15-108"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Well-known draft breeds include the <a href="/wiki/Belgian_Draught" title="Belgian Draught">Belgian</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Clydesdale_horse" title="Clydesdale horse">Clydesdale</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Catalog15_108-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Catalog15-108"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some, like the <a href="/wiki/Percheron" title="Percheron">Percheron</a>, are lighter and livelier, developed to pull carriages or to plow large fields in drier climates.<sup id="cite_ref-Guide87_109-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Guide87-109"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Others, such as the <a href="/wiki/Shire_horse" title="Shire horse">Shire</a>, are slower and more powerful, bred to plow fields with heavy, clay-based soils.<sup id="cite_ref-Ens124_110-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ens124-110"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The cold-blooded group also includes some pony breeds.<sup id="cite_ref-Bennett7_111-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bennett7-111"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>"<a href="/wiki/Warmblood" title="Warmblood">Warmblood</a>" breeds, such as the <a href="/wiki/Trakehner" title="Trakehner">Trakehner</a> or <a href="/wiki/Hanoverian_horse" title="Hanoverian horse">Hanoverian</a>, developed when European carriage and <a href="/wiki/Horses_in_warfare" title="Horses in warfare">war horses</a> were crossed with Arabians or Thoroughbreds, producing a riding horse with more refinement than a draft horse, but greater size and milder temperament than a lighter breed.<sup id="cite_ref-112" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-112"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Certain <a href="/wiki/Pony" title="Pony">pony</a> breeds with warmblood characteristics have been developed for smaller riders.<sup id="cite_ref-113" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-113"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Warmbloods are considered a "light horse" or "riding horse".<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger71_102-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger71-102"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Today, the term "Warmblood" refers to a specific subset of <a href="/wiki/Sport_horse" title="Sport horse">sport horse</a> breeds that are used for competition in <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a> and <a href="/wiki/Show_jumping" title="Show jumping">show jumping</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Lyons231_114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lyons231-114"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Strictly speaking, the term "<a href="/wiki/Warmblood" title="Warmblood">warm blood</a>" refers to any <a href="/wiki/Crossbreeding" class="mw-redirect" title="Crossbreeding">cross</a> between cold-blooded and hot-blooded breeds.<sup id="cite_ref-Belknap523_115-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Belknap523-115"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Examples include breeds such as the <a href="/wiki/Irish_Draught" title="Irish Draught">Irish Draught</a> or the <a href="/wiki/Cleveland_Bay" title="Cleveland Bay">Cleveland Bay</a>. The term was once used to refer to breeds of light riding horse other than Thoroughbreds or Arabians, such as the <a href="/wiki/Morgan_horse" title="Morgan horse">Morgan horse</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Catalog18_104-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Catalog18-104"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sleep_patterns">Sleep patterns</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Horse_behavior#Sleep_patterns" title="Horse behavior">Horse sleep patterns</a> and <a href="/wiki/Sleep_in_non-human_animals" class="mw-redirect" title="Sleep in non-human animals">Sleep in non-humans</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Biandintz_eta_zaldiak_-_modified2.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Two horses in a pasture, one is standing beside the other that is laying down." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Biandintz_eta_zaldiak_-_modified2.jpg/250px-Biandintz_eta_zaldiak_-_modified2.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="173" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Biandintz_eta_zaldiak_-_modified2.jpg/500px-Biandintz_eta_zaldiak_-_modified2.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1850" data-file-height="1282"></a><figcaption>When horses lie down to sleep, others in the herd remain standing, awake, or in a light doze, keeping watch.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses are able to sleep both standing up and lying down. In an adaptation from life in the wild, horses are able to enter light sleep by using a "<a href="/wiki/Stay_apparatus" title="Stay apparatus">stay apparatus</a>" in their legs, allowing them to doze without collapsing.<sup id="cite_ref-116" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-116"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses sleep better when in groups because some animals will sleep while others stand guard to watch for predators. A horse kept alone will not sleep well because its <a href="/wiki/Instinct" title="Instinct">instinct</a> is to keep a constant eye out for danger.<sup id="cite_ref-Horse_sleep_pt._2_117-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Horse_sleep_pt._2-117"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Unlike humans, horses do not sleep in a solid, unbroken period of time, but take many short periods of rest. Horses spend four to fifteen hours a day in standing rest, and from a few minutes to several hours lying down. Total sleep time in a 24-hour period may range from several minutes to a couple of hours,<sup id="cite_ref-Horse_sleep_pt._2_117-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Horse_sleep_pt._2-117"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> mostly in short intervals of about 15 minutes each.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger310_118-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger310-118"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The average sleep time of a domestic horse is said to be 2.9 hours per day.<sup id="cite_ref-119" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-119"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses must lie down to reach <a href="/wiki/Rapid_eye_movement_sleep" title="Rapid eye movement sleep">REM sleep</a>. They only have to lie down for an hour or two every few days to meet their minimum REM sleep requirements.<sup id="cite_ref-Horse_sleep_pt._2_117-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Horse_sleep_pt._2-117"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, if a horse is never allowed to lie down, after several days it will become sleep-deprived, and in rare cases may suddenly collapse because it slips, involuntarily, into REM sleep while still standing.<sup id="cite_ref-120" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-120"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This condition differs from <a href="/wiki/Narcolepsy" title="Narcolepsy">narcolepsy</a>, although horses may also suffer from that disorder.<sup id="cite_ref-Smith_121-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Smith-121"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Taxonomy_and_evolution">Taxonomy and evolution</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Evolution_of_the_horse" title="Evolution of the horse">Evolution of the horse</a>, <a href="/wiki/Equus_(genus)" title="Equus (genus)">Equus (genus)</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Equidae" title="Equidae">Equidae</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Equine_evolution.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Equine_evolution.jpg/500px-Equine_evolution.jpg" decoding="async" width="500" height="427" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.5" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Equine_evolution.jpg/960px-Equine_evolution.jpg 2x" data-file-width="9623" data-file-height="8223"></a><figcaption>A diagram of horse evolution, showing size development, biometrical changes in the cranium and reduction of toes (left forefoot)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The horse <a href="/wiki/Adaptation" title="Adaptation">adapted</a> to survive in areas of wide-open terrain with sparse vegetation, surviving in an <a href="/wiki/Ecosystem" title="Ecosystem">ecosystem</a> where other large grazing animals, especially <a href="/wiki/Ruminant" title="Ruminant">ruminants</a>, could not.<sup id="cite_ref-Budiansky_122-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Budiansky-122"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses and other equids are <a href="/wiki/Odd-toed_ungulate" class="mw-redirect" title="Odd-toed ungulate">odd-toed ungulates</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Order_(biology)" title="Order (biology)">order</a> <a href="/wiki/Perissodactyla" title="Perissodactyla">Perissodactyla</a>, a group of mammals dominant during the <a href="/wiki/Tertiary_(period)" class="mw-redirect" title="Tertiary (period)">Tertiary</a>. In the past, this order contained 14 <a href="/wiki/Family_(biology)" title="Family (biology)">families</a>, but only three—<a href="/wiki/Equidae" title="Equidae">Equidae</a> (the horse and related species), <a href="/wiki/Tapiridae" class="mw-redirect" title="Tapiridae">Tapiridae</a> (the <a href="/wiki/Tapir" title="Tapir">tapir</a>), and <a href="/wiki/Rhinocerotidae" class="mw-redirect" title="Rhinocerotidae">Rhinocerotidae</a> (the <a href="/wiki/Rhinoceros" title="Rhinoceros">rhinoceroses</a>)—have survived to the present day.<sup id="cite_ref-123" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-123"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The earliest known member of the family Equidae was the <i><a href="/wiki/Hyracotherium" title="Hyracotherium">Hyracotherium</a></i>, which lived between 45 and 55 million years ago, during the <a href="/wiki/Eocene" title="Eocene">Eocene</a> period. It had 4 toes on each front foot, and 3 toes on each back foot.<sup id="cite_ref-124" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-124"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The extra toe on the front feet soon disappeared with the <i><a href="/wiki/Mesohippus" title="Mesohippus">Mesohippus</a></i>, which lived 32 to 37 million years ago.<sup id="cite_ref-125" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-125"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Over time, the extra side toes shrank in size until they vanished. All that remains of them in modern horses is a set of small <a href="/wiki/Vestigiality" title="Vestigiality">vestigial</a> bones on the leg below the knee,<sup id="cite_ref-Natural_126-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Natural-126"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> known informally as splint bones.<sup id="cite_ref-127" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-127"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Their legs lengthened as their toes disappeared until they were a hooved animal capable of running at great speed.<sup id="cite_ref-Natural_126-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Natural-126"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> By about 5 million years ago, the modern <i>Equus</i> had evolved, and it spread to the Old World from North America during the <a href="/wiki/Pleistocene" title="Pleistocene">Pleistocene</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Florida_128-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Florida-128"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Equid teeth also evolved from browsing on soft, tropical plants to adapt to browsing of drier plant material, then to grazing of tougher plains grasses. Thus, proto-horses changed from leaf-eating forest-dwellers to grass-eating inhabitants of semi-arid regions worldwide, including the <a href="/wiki/Steppe" title="Steppe">steppes</a> of Eurasia and the <a href="/wiki/Great_Plains" title="Great Plains">Great Plains</a> of North America.
</p><p>By about 15,000 years ago, <i>Equus ferus</i> was a widespread <a href="/wiki/Holarctic" class="mw-redirect" title="Holarctic">holarctic</a> species. Horse bones from this time period, the late <a href="/wiki/Pleistocene" title="Pleistocene">Pleistocene</a>, are found in Europe, Eurasia, <a href="/wiki/Beringia" title="Beringia">Beringia</a>, and North America.<sup id="cite_ref-Weinstock_129-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Weinstock-129"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Yet between 10,000 and 7,600 years ago, the horse became extinct in North America.<sup id="cite_ref-VilaWidespreadOrigins_130-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-VilaWidespreadOrigins-130"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-IberianOrigins_131-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IberianOrigins-131"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Haile_132-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Haile-132"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The reasons for this extinction are not fully known, but one theory notes that extinction in North America paralleled human arrival.<sup id="cite_ref-Buck_133-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Buck-133"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another theory points to <a href="/wiki/Climate_change" title="Climate change">climate change</a>, noting that approximately 12,500 years ago, the grasses characteristic of a <a href="/wiki/Steppe" title="Steppe">steppe</a> ecosystem gave way to shrub <a href="/wiki/Tundra" title="Tundra">tundra</a>, which was covered with unpalatable plants.<sup id="cite_ref-LeQuire_134-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LeQuire-134"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Wild_species_surviving_into_modern_times">Wild species surviving into modern times</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Wild_horse" title="Wild horse">Wild horse</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:France_Loz%C3%A8re_Causse_M%C3%A9jean_Chevaux_de_Przewalski_20.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Three tan-colored horses with upright manes. Two horses nip and paw at each other, while the third moves towards the camera. They stand in an open, rocky grassland, with forests in the distance." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/France_Loz%C3%A8re_Causse_M%C3%A9jean_Chevaux_de_Przewalski_20.jpg/250px-France_Loz%C3%A8re_Causse_M%C3%A9jean_Chevaux_de_Przewalski_20.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/France_Loz%C3%A8re_Causse_M%C3%A9jean_Chevaux_de_Przewalski_20.jpg/500px-France_Loz%C3%A8re_Causse_M%C3%A9jean_Chevaux_de_Przewalski_20.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2816" data-file-height="2112"></a><figcaption>A small herd of Przewalski's horses</figcaption></figure>
<p>A truly wild horse is a species or subspecies with no ancestors that were ever successfully domesticated. Therefore, most "wild" horses today are actually <a href="/wiki/Feral_horse" title="Feral horse">feral horses</a>, animals that escaped or were turned loose from domestic herds and the descendants of those animals.<sup id="cite_ref-Olsen46_135-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olsen46-135"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Only two wild subspecies, the <a href="/wiki/Tarpan" title="Tarpan">tarpan</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Przewalski%27s_horse" title="Przewalski's horse">Przewalski's horse</a>, survived into recorded history and only the latter survives today.
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Przewalski%27s_horse" title="Przewalski's horse">Przewalski's horse</a> (<i>Equus ferus przewalskii</i>), named after the Russian explorer <a href="/wiki/Nikolai_Przhevalsky" class="mw-redirect" title="Nikolai Przhevalsky">Nikolai Przhevalsky</a>, is a rare Asian animal. It is also known as the Mongolian wild horse; <a href="/wiki/Mongolia" title="Mongolia">Mongolian</a> people know it as the <i>taki</i>, and the <a href="/wiki/Kyrgyz_people" title="Kyrgyz people">Kyrgyz people</a> call it a <i>kirtag</i>. The subspecies was presumed extinct in the wild between 1969 and 1992, while a small breeding population survived in zoos around the world. In 1992, it was reestablished in the wild by the conservation efforts of numerous zoos.<sup id="cite_ref-136" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-136"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Today, a small wild breeding population exists in Mongolia.<sup id="cite_ref-137" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-137"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Dohner298_138-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dohner298-138"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There are additional animals still maintained at zoos throughout the world.
</p><p>Their status as a truly wild horse was called into question when domestic horses of the 5,000-year-old <a href="/wiki/Botai_culture" title="Botai culture">Botai culture</a> of Central Asia were found to be more closely related to Przewalski's horses than to <i>E. f. caballus</i>. The study raised the possibility that modern Przewalski's horses could be the feral descendants of the domestic Botai horses. The study concluded that the Botai animals appear to have been an independent domestication attempt that was unsuccessful in terms of genetic markers being carried through to modern domesticated equines. However, the question of whether all Przewalski's horses descend from this population is also unresolved, as only one of seven modern Przewalski's horses in the study shared this ancestry. It may also be that both the Botai horses and the modern Przewalski's horses descend separately from the same ancient wild Przewalski's horse population.<sup id="cite_ref-Pennisi_139-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Pennisi-139"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Orlando_140-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Orlando-140"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-141" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-141"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The tarpan or European wild horse (<i>Equus ferus ferus</i>) was found in Europe and much of Asia. It survived into the historical era, but became <a href="/wiki/Extinction" title="Extinction">extinct</a> in 1909, when the last captive died in a Russian zoo.<sup id="cite_ref-Dohner300_142-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dohner300-142"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Thus, the genetic line was lost. Attempts have been made to recreate the tarpan,<sup id="cite_ref-Dohner300_142-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dohner300-142"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-OSU_143-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OSU-143"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-144" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-144"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which resulted in horses with outward physical similarities, but nonetheless descended from domesticated ancestors and not true wild horses.
</p><p>Periodically, populations of horses in isolated areas are speculated to be <a href="/wiki/Relict_(biology)" title="Relict (biology)">relict</a> populations of wild horses, but generally have been proven to be feral or domestic. For example, the <a href="/wiki/Riwoche_horse" title="Riwoche horse">Riwoche horse</a> of Tibet was proposed as such,<sup id="cite_ref-Dohner298_138-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dohner298-138"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> but testing did not reveal genetic differences from domesticated horses.<sup id="cite_ref-Tibet_145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tibet-145"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Similarly, the <a href="/wiki/Sorraia" title="Sorraia">Sorraia</a> of Portugal was proposed as a direct descendant of the tarpan on the basis of shared characteristics,<sup id="cite_ref-Royo_146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Royo-146"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Edwards_147-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Edwards-147"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> but genetic studies have shown that the Sorraia is more closely related to other horse breeds, and that the outward similarity is an unreliable measure of relatedness.<sup id="cite_ref-Royo_146-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Royo-146"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Lira_148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lira-148"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Other_modern_equids">Other modern equids</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Equus_(genus)" title="Equus (genus)">Equus (genus)</a></div>
<p>Besides the horse, there are six other species of <a href="/wiki/Genus" title="Genus">genus</a> <i>Equus</i> in the Equidae <a href="/wiki/Family_(biology)" title="Family (biology)">family</a>. These are the ass or <a href="/wiki/Donkey" title="Donkey">donkey</a>, <i>Equus asinus</i>; the <a href="/wiki/Mountain_zebra" title="Mountain zebra">mountain zebra</a>, <i>Equus zebra</i>; <a href="/wiki/Plains_zebra" title="Plains zebra">plains zebra</a>, <i>Equus quagga</i>; <a href="/wiki/Gr%C3%A9vy%27s_zebra" title="Grévy's zebra">Grévy's zebra</a>, <i>Equus grevyi</i>; the <a href="/wiki/Kiang" title="Kiang">kiang</a>, <i>Equus kiang</i>; and the <a href="/wiki/Onager" title="Onager">onager</a>, <i>Equus hemionus</i>.<sup id="cite_ref-149" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-149"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses can <a href="/wiki/Crossbreed" title="Crossbreed">crossbreed</a> with other members of their genus. The most common <a href="/wiki/Hybrid_(biology)" title="Hybrid (biology)">hybrid</a> is the <a href="/wiki/Mule" title="Mule">mule</a>, a cross between a "jack" (male donkey) and a <a href="/wiki/Mare" title="Mare">mare</a>. A related hybrid, a <a href="/wiki/Hinny" title="Hinny">hinny</a>, is a cross between a stallion and a "jenny" (female donkey).<sup id="cite_ref-150" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-150"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Other hybrids include the <a href="/wiki/Zorse" class="mw-redirect" title="Zorse">zorse</a>, a cross between a zebra and a horse.<sup id="cite_ref-151" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-151"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> With rare exceptions, most hybrids are <a href="/wiki/Infertility" title="Infertility">sterile</a> and cannot reproduce.<sup id="cite_ref-152" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-152"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Domestication_and_history">Domestication and history</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/History_of_horse_domestication_theories" title="History of horse domestication theories">History of horse domestication theories</a> and <a href="/wiki/Domestication_of_the_horse" title="Domestication of the horse">Domestication of the horse</a></div><p><span class="anchor" id="Domestication"></span></p><figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Bhimbetka_rock_paintng1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Bhimbetka_rock_paintng1.jpg/250px-Bhimbetka_rock_paintng1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="159" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Bhimbetka_rock_paintng1.jpg/500px-Bhimbetka_rock_paintng1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="885" data-file-height="563"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Bhimbetka_rock_shelters" title="Bhimbetka rock shelters">Bhimbetka</a> rock painting showing a man riding on a horse, India</figcaption></figure>
<p>Domestication of the horse most likely took place in central Asia prior to 3500 BCE. Two major sources of information are used to determine where and when the horse was first domesticated and how the domesticated horse spread around the world. The first source is based on <a href="/wiki/Paleontology" title="Paleontology">palaeological</a> and archaeological discoveries; the second source is a comparison of DNA obtained from modern horses to that from bones and teeth of ancient horse remains.
</p><p>The earliest archaeological evidence for attempted domestication of the horse comes from sites in the <a href="/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe" title="Eurasian Steppe">Western Eurasian Steppes</a>, particularly the lower <a href="/wiki/Volga_region" title="Volga region">Volga</a>-<a href="/wiki/Donbas" title="Donbas">Don</a> region, dating to approximately 4000–3500 BCE.<sup id="cite_ref-153" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-153"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-154" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-154"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>  However the horses domesticated at the Botai culture in Kazakhstan were Przewalski's horses and not the ancestors of modern horses.<sup id="cite_ref-155" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-155"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-librado_156-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-librado-156"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>By 3000 BCE, the horse was completely domesticated, and by 2000 BCE, there was a sharp increase in the number of horse bones found in human settlements in northwestern Europe, indicating the spread of domesticated horses throughout the continent.<sup id="cite_ref-157" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-157"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The most recent, but most irrefutable evidence of domestication comes from sites where horse remains were interred with chariots in graves of the Indo-European <a href="/wiki/Sintashta" title="Sintashta">Sintashta</a> and <a href="/wiki/Petrovka_settlement" class="mw-redirect" title="Petrovka settlement">Petrovka</a> cultures <abbr title="circa">c.</abbr> 2100 BCE.<sup id="cite_ref-Kuznetsov2006_158-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kuznetsov2006-158"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The genetic makeup of modern domesticated horses all carry the DOM2 gene, which first emerged in West Eurasia towards the end of the 3rd millennium BCE.<sup id="cite_ref-librado_156-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-librado-156"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, the DOM2 had limited genetic influence of <a href="/wiki/Wild_type" title="Wild type">wild type</a> genes during this period.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_159-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-159"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The rapid spread of DOM2 occurred around 2200 BCE, coinciding with the <a href="/wiki/Sintashta_culture" title="Sintashta culture">Sintashta culture</a> and development of spoke-wheeled <a href="/wiki/Chariot" title="Chariot">chariots</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_159-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-159"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This demonstrates that certain adaptations were strongly selected due to <a href="/wiki/Equestrianism" title="Equestrianism">riding</a>, and that <a href="/wiki/Horse_culture" title="Horse culture">equestrian material culture</a> that spread with the horse itself.<sup id="cite_ref-160" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-160"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-librado_156-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-librado-156"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Domestication is also studied by using the genetic material of present-day horses and comparing it with the genetic material present in the bones and teeth of horse remains found in archaeological and palaeological excavations. The variation in the genetic material shows that very few wild stallions contributed to the domestic horse,<sup id="cite_ref-Lau_161-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lau-161"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Lindgren2004_162-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lindgren2004-162"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> while many mares were part of early domesticated herds.<sup id="cite_ref-Lira_148-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lira-148"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Vila2001_163-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Vila2001-163"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Cai_164-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cai-164"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This is reflected in the difference in genetic variation between the DNA that is passed on along the paternal, or sire line (<a href="/wiki/Y-chromosome" class="mw-redirect" title="Y-chromosome">Y-chromosome</a>) versus that passed on along the maternal, or dam line (<a href="/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA" title="Mitochondrial DNA">mitochondrial DNA</a>). There are very low levels of Y-chromosome variability,<sup id="cite_ref-Lau_161-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lau-161"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Lindgren2004_162-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lindgren2004-162"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> but a great deal of genetic variation in mitochondrial DNA.<sup id="cite_ref-Lira_148-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lira-148"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Vila2001_163-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Vila2001-163"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Cai_164-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cai-164"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There is also regional variation in mitochondrial DNA due to the inclusion of wild mares in domestic herds.<sup id="cite_ref-Lira_148-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lira-148"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Vila2001_163-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Vila2001-163"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Cai_164-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cai-164"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-165" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-165"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another characteristic of domestication is an increase in coat color variation.<sup id="cite_ref-166" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-166"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In horses, this increased dramatically between 5000 and 3000 BCE.<sup id="cite_ref-coatColor_167-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-coatColor-167"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Before the availability of DNA techniques to resolve the questions related to the domestication of the horse, various hypotheses were proposed. One classification was based on body types and conformation, suggesting the presence of four basic prototypes that had adapted to their environment prior to domestication.<sup id="cite_ref-Bennett7_111-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bennett7-111"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another hypothesis held that the four prototypes originated from a single wild species and that all different body types were entirely a result of <a href="/wiki/Selective_breeding" title="Selective breeding">selective breeding</a> after domestication.<sup id="cite_ref-168" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-168"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, the lack of a detectable substructure  in the horse has resulted in a rejection of both hypotheses.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Feral_populations">Feral populations</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Feral_horse" title="Feral horse">Feral horse</a></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Feral" title="Feral">Feral</a> horses are born and live in the wild, but are descended from domesticated animals.<sup id="cite_ref-Olsen46_135-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olsen46-135"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Many populations of <a href="/wiki/Feral_horse" title="Feral horse">feral horses</a> exist throughout the world.<sup id="cite_ref-169" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-169"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-170" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-170"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Studies of feral herds have provided useful insights into the behavior of prehistoric horses,<sup id="cite_ref-171" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-171"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> as well as greater understanding of the instincts and behaviors that drive horses that live in domesticated conditions.<sup id="cite_ref-172" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-172"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>There are also <a href="/wiki/Semi-feral" title="Semi-feral">semi-feral</a> horses in many parts of the world, such as <a href="/wiki/Dartmoor" title="Dartmoor">Dartmoor</a> and the <a href="/wiki/New_Forest" title="New Forest">New Forest</a> in the UK, where the animals are all privately owned but live for significant amounts of time in "wild" conditions on undeveloped, often public, lands. Owners of such animals often pay a fee for grazing rights.<sup id="cite_ref-173" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-173"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Fear75_174-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fear75-174"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Breeds">Breeds</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Horse_breed" title="Horse breed">Horse breed</a>, <a href="/wiki/List_of_horse_breeds" title="List of horse breeds">List of horse breeds</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Horse_breeding" title="Horse breeding">Horse breeding</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Brockhaus_and_Efron_Encyclopedic_Dictionary_b35_043-0.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="a sepia-toned engraving from an old book, showing 11 horses of different breeds and sizes in nine different illustrations" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Brockhaus_and_Efron_Encyclopedic_Dictionary_b35_043-0.jpg/500px-Brockhaus_and_Efron_Encyclopedic_Dictionary_b35_043-0.jpg" decoding="async" width="500" height="386" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.5" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Brockhaus_and_Efron_Encyclopedic_Dictionary_b35_043-0.jpg/960px-Brockhaus_and_Efron_Encyclopedic_Dictionary_b35_043-0.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3298" data-file-height="2545"></a><figcaption>An illustration of assorted breeds; slim, light hotbloods, medium-sized warmbloods and draft and pony-type coldblood breeds</figcaption></figure>
<p>The concept of <a href="/wiki/Purebred" title="Purebred">purebred</a> bloodstock and a controlled, written <a href="/wiki/Breed_registry" title="Breed registry">breed registry</a> has come to be particularly significant and important in modern times. Sometimes purebred horses are incorrectly or inaccurately called "thoroughbreds". <a href="/wiki/Thoroughbred" title="Thoroughbred">Thoroughbred</a> is a specific breed of horse, while a "purebred" is a horse (or any other animal) with a defined <a href="/wiki/Pedigree_chart" title="Pedigree chart">pedigree</a> recognized by a breed registry.<sup id="cite_ref-Ensminger424_175-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ensminger424-175"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horse breeds are groups of horses with distinctive characteristics that are transmitted consistently to their offspring, such as <a href="/wiki/Equine_conformation" title="Equine conformation">conformation</a>, color, performance ability, or disposition.<sup id="cite_ref-176" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-176"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>These inherited traits result from a combination of natural crosses and <a href="/wiki/Artificial_selection" class="mw-redirect" title="Artificial selection">artificial selection</a> methods. Horses have been <a href="/wiki/Selective_breeding" title="Selective breeding">selectively bred</a> since their domestication. An early example of people who practiced selective <a href="/wiki/Horse_breeding" title="Horse breeding">horse breeding</a> was the <a href="/wiki/Bedouin" title="Bedouin">Bedouin</a>, who had a reputation for careful practices, keeping extensive pedigrees of their <a href="/wiki/Arabian_horse" title="Arabian horse">Arabian horses</a> and placing great value upon pure bloodlines.<sup id="cite_ref-177" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-177"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> These pedigrees were originally transmitted via an <a href="/wiki/Oral_tradition" title="Oral tradition">oral tradition</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-178" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-178"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Breeds developed due to a need for "form to function", the necessity to develop certain characteristics in order to perform a particular type of work.<sup id="cite_ref-Sponenberg155_179-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sponenberg155-179"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Thus, a powerful but refined breed such as the Andalusian developed as riding horses with an aptitude for <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Sponenberg155_179-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sponenberg155-179"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Heavy draft horses were developed out of a need to perform demanding <a href="/wiki/Farm" title="Farm">farm</a> work and pull heavy wagons.<sup id="cite_ref-180" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-180"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Other horse breeds had been developed specifically for light agricultural work, carriage and road work, various sport disciplines, or simply as pets.<sup id="cite_ref-Spon162_181-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Spon162-181"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Some breeds developed through centuries of crossing other breeds, while others descended from a single <a href="/wiki/Foundation_stock" title="Foundation stock">foundation sire</a>, or other limited or restricted foundation bloodstock. One of the earliest formal registries was <a href="/wiki/General_Stud_Book" title="General Stud Book">General Stud Book</a> for Thoroughbreds, which began in 1791 and traced back to the foundation bloodstock for the breed.<sup id="cite_ref-182" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-182"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There are more than 300 horse breeds in the world today.<sup id="cite_ref-183" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-183"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Interaction_with_humans">Interaction with humans</h2></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Murron-Ryhti_pulling.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Murron-Ryhti_pulling.png/250px-Murron-Ryhti_pulling.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="211" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Murron-Ryhti_pulling.png/500px-Murron-Ryhti_pulling.png 2x" data-file-width="2063" data-file-height="1745"></a><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Finnhorse" title="Finnhorse">Finnhorse</a> pulling a heavy wagon</figcaption></figure>
<p>Worldwide, horses play a role within human cultures and have done so for millennia. Horses are used for leisure activities, sports, and working purposes. The <a href="/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization" title="Food and Agriculture Organization">Food and Agriculture Organization</a> (FAO) estimates that in 2008, there were almost 59,000,000 horses in the world, with around 33,500,000 in the Americas, 13,800,000 in Asia, and 6,300,000 in Europe and smaller portions in Africa and Oceania. There are estimated to be 9,500,000 horses in the United States alone.<sup id="cite_ref-184" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-184"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/American_Horse_Council" title="American Horse Council">American Horse Council</a> estimates that horse-related activities have a direct impact on the economy of the United States of over $39 billion supporting 1.4 billion jobs full time, and when indirect spending is considered, the impact is over $102 billion.<sup id="cite_ref-185" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-185"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In a 2004 "poll" conducted by <a href="/wiki/Animal_Planet" title="Animal Planet">Animal Planet</a>, more than 50,000 viewers from 73 countries voted for the horse as the world's 4th favorite animal.<sup id="cite_ref-IOL_186-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IOL-186"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Communication between human and horse is paramount in any equestrian activity;<sup id="cite_ref-Olsen_187-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olsen-187"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> to aid this process, horses are usually ridden with a <a href="/wiki/Saddle" title="Saddle">saddle</a> on their backs to assist the rider with balance and positioning, and a <a href="/wiki/Bridle" title="Bridle">bridle</a> or related headgear to assist the rider in maintaining control.<sup id="cite_ref-188" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-188"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sometimes horses are ridden without a saddle,<sup id="cite_ref-189" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-189"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and occasionally, horses are trained to perform without a bridle or other headgear.<sup id="cite_ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Many horses are also <a href="/wiki/Driving_(horse)" title="Driving (horse)">driven</a>, which requires a harness, bridle, and some type of <a href="/wiki/Horse-drawn_vehicle" title="Horse-drawn vehicle">vehicle</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sport">Sport</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Equestrianism" title="Equestrianism">Equestrianism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_racing" title="Horse racing">Horse racing</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_training" title="Horse training">Horse training</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Horse_tack" title="Horse tack">Horse tack</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Beijing2008_HOKETSU_Hiroshi.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A chestnut (reddish-brown) horse being ridden by a rider in a black coat and top hat. They are stopped in a riding arena with the rider tipping his hat." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Beijing2008_HOKETSU_Hiroshi.JPG/250px-Beijing2008_HOKETSU_Hiroshi.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="202" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Beijing2008_HOKETSU_Hiroshi.JPG/500px-Beijing2008_HOKETSU_Hiroshi.JPG 2x" data-file-width="547" data-file-height="442"></a><figcaption>A horse and rider in <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a> competition at the <a href="/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_Summer_Olympics" class="mw-redirect" title="Equestrian at the Summer Olympics">Olympics</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Historically, equestrians honed their skills through games and races. Equestrian sports provided entertainment for crowds and honed the excellent horsemanship that was needed in battle. Many sports, such as <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a>, <a href="/wiki/Eventing" title="Eventing">eventing</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Show_jumping" title="Show jumping">show jumping</a>, have origins in <a href="/wiki/Horses_in_warfare" title="Horses in warfare">military training</a>, which were focused on control and balance of both horse and rider. Other sports, such as <a href="/wiki/Rodeo" title="Rodeo">rodeo</a>, developed from practical skills such as those needed on working <a href="/wiki/Ranch" title="Ranch">ranches</a> and <a href="/wiki/Station_(Australian_agriculture)" title="Station (Australian agriculture)">stations</a>. Sport hunting from horseback evolved from earlier practical hunting techniques.<sup id="cite_ref-Olsen_187-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olsen-187"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Horse racing of all types evolved from impromptu competitions between riders or drivers. All forms of competition, requiring demanding and specialized skills from both horse and rider, resulted in the systematic development of specialized breeds and equipment for each sport. The popularity of equestrian sports through the centuries has resulted in the preservation of skills that would otherwise have disappeared after horses stopped being used in combat.<sup id="cite_ref-Olsen_187-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olsen-187"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses are trained to be ridden or driven in a variety of sporting competitions. Examples include <a href="/wiki/Show_jumping" title="Show jumping">show jumping</a>, <a href="/wiki/Dressage" title="Dressage">dressage</a>, three-day <a href="/wiki/Eventing" title="Eventing">eventing</a>, <a href="/wiki/Combined_driving" title="Combined driving">competitive driving</a>, <a href="/wiki/Endurance_riding" title="Endurance riding">endurance riding</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gymkhana_(equestrian)" title="Gymkhana (equestrian)">gymkhana</a>, rodeos, and <a href="/wiki/Fox_hunting" title="Fox hunting">fox hunting</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Horse_show" title="Horse show">Horse shows</a>, which have their origins in medieval European fairs, are held around the world. They host a huge range of classes, covering all of the mounted and harness disciplines, as well as <a href="/wiki/Halter_(horse_show)" title="Halter (horse show)">"In-hand"</a> classes where the horses are led, rather than ridden, to be evaluated on their conformation. The method of judging varies with the discipline, but winning usually depends on style and ability of both horse and rider.<sup id="cite_ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Sports such as <a href="/wiki/Polo" title="Polo">polo</a> do not judge the horse itself, but rather use the horse as a partner for human competitors as a necessary part of the game. Although the horse requires specialized training to participate, the details of its performance are not judged, only the result of the rider's actions—be it getting a ball through a goal or some other task.<sup id="cite_ref-Edwards360_194-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Edwards360-194"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Examples of these sports of partnership between human and horse include <a href="/wiki/Jousting" title="Jousting">jousting</a>, in which the main goal is for one rider to unseat the other,<sup id="cite_ref-195" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-195"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Buzkashi" title="Buzkashi">buzkashi</a>, a team game played throughout <a href="/wiki/Central_Asia" title="Central Asia">Central Asia</a>, the aim being to capture a goat carcass while on horseback.<sup id="cite_ref-Edwards360_194-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Edwards360-194"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horse racing is an equestrian sport and major international industry, watched in almost every nation of the world. There are three types: "flat" racing; <a href="/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing)" title="Steeplechase (horse racing)">steeplechasing</a>, i.e. racing over jumps; and harness racing, where horses trot or pace while pulling a driver in a small, light cart known as a <a href="/wiki/Sulky" title="Sulky">sulky</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-196" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-196"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A major part of horse racing's economic importance lies in the <a href="/wiki/Gambling#Parimutuel_betting" title="Gambling">gambling</a> associated with it.<sup id="cite_ref-197" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-197"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Work">Work</h3></div>
