horse" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Policja_konna_Pozna%C5%84.jpg/250px-Policja_konna_Pozna%C5%84.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="316" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Policja_konna_Pozna%C5%84.jpg/330px-Policja_konna_Pozna%C5%84.jpg 2x" data-file-width="619" data-file-height="783"></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">A mounted police officer in Poland</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>There are certain jobs that horses do very well, and no technology has yet developed to fully replace them. For example, <a href="/wiki/Mounted_police" title="Mounted police">mounted police</a> horses are still effective for certain types of patrol duties and crowd control.<sup id="cite_ref-198" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-198"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Cattle ranches still require riders on horseback to round up cattle that are scattered across remote, rugged terrain.<sup id="cite_ref-199" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-199"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Search_and_rescue" title="Search and rescue">Search and rescue</a> organizations in some countries depend upon <a href="/wiki/Mounted_search_and_rescue" title="Mounted search and rescue">mounted</a> teams to locate people, particularly hikers and children, and to provide disaster relief assistance.<sup id="cite_ref-200" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-200"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Horses can also be used in areas where it is necessary to avoid vehicular disruption to delicate soil, such as nature reserves. They may also be the only form of transport allowed in <a href="/wiki/Wilderness_area" class="mw-redirect" title="Wilderness area">wilderness areas</a>. Horses are quieter than motorized vehicles. <a href="/wiki/Law_enforcement_officer" title="Law enforcement officer">Law enforcement officers</a> such as <a href="/wiki/Park_ranger" title="Park ranger">park rangers</a> or <a href="/wiki/Game_warden" class="mw-redirect" title="Game warden">game wardens</a> may use horses for patrols, and horses or mules may also be used for clearing trails or other work in areas of rough terrain where vehicles are less effective.<sup id="cite_ref-201" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-201"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Although machinery has replaced horses in many parts of the world, an estimated 100 million horses, donkeys and mules are still used for agriculture and transportation in less developed areas. This number includes around 27 million <a href="/wiki/Working_animal" title="Working animal">working animals</a> in Africa alone.<sup id="cite_ref-202" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-202"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some land management practices, such as cultivating and logging, can be efficiently performed with horses. In agriculture, less fossil fuel is used, and increased environmental conservation occurs over time with the use of <a href="/wiki/Working_animal" title="Working animal">draft animals</a> such as horses.<sup id="cite_ref-203" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-203"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-204" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-204"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Logging with horses can result in reduced damage to soil structure and less damage to trees due to more selective logging.<sup id="cite_ref-205" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-205"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Warfare">Warfare</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Horses_in_warfare" title="Horses in warfare">Horses in warfare</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-073-17,_T%C3%BCrkische_Kavallerie_s%C3%BCdlich_von_Jerusalem.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Black-and-white photo of mounted soldiers with Middle Eastern headwraps, carrying rifles, walking down a road away from the camera" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-073-17%2C_T%C3%BCrkische_Kavallerie_s%C3%BCdlich_von_Jerusalem.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-073-17%2C_T%C3%BCrkische_Kavallerie_s%C3%BCdlich_von_Jerusalem.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="175" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-073-17%2C_T%C3%BCrkische_Kavallerie_s%C3%BCdlich_von_Jerusalem.jpg/500px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-073-17%2C_T%C3%BCrkische_Kavallerie_s%C3%BCdlich_von_Jerusalem.jpg 2x" data-file-width="789" data-file-height="553"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Ottoman_Empire" title="Ottoman Empire">Ottoman</a> cavalry, 1917</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses have been used in warfare for most of recorded history. The first archaeological evidence of horses used in warfare dates to between 4000 and 3000 BCE,<sup id="cite_ref-Science_Show_206-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Science_Show-206"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and the use of horses in warfare was widespread by the end of the <a href="/wiki/Bronze_Age" title="Bronze Age">Bronze Age</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Hartwick_207-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hartwick-207"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Whitaker30_208-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Whitaker30-208"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Although mechanization has largely replaced the horse as a weapon of war, horses are still seen today in limited military uses, mostly for ceremonial purposes, or for reconnaissance and transport activities in areas of rough terrain where motorized vehicles are ineffective. Horses have been used in the 21st century by the <a href="/wiki/Janjaweed" title="Janjaweed">Janjaweed</a> militias in the <a href="/wiki/War_in_Darfur" title="War in Darfur">War in Darfur</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-209" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-209"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Entertainment_and_culture">Entertainment and culture</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Horse_symbolism" title="Horse symbolism">Horse symbolism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horses_in_art" title="Horses in art">Horses in art</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Horse_worship" title="Horse worship">Horse worship</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Hayagreeva.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Hayagreeva.jpg/250px-Hayagreeva.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="351" style="--mw-file-upright: 0.75" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0b/Hayagreeva.jpg 2x" data-file-width="487" data-file-height="683"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Hayagriva" title="Hayagriva">Hayagriva</a>, the horse-headed deity in <a href="/wiki/Hinduism" title="Hinduism">Hinduism</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Modern horses are often used to reenact many of their historical work purposes. Horses are used, complete with equipment that is authentic or a meticulously recreated replica, in various live action <a href="/wiki/Historical_reenactment" title="Historical reenactment">historical reenactments</a> of specific periods of history, especially recreations of famous battles.<sup id="cite_ref-210" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-210"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horses are also used to preserve cultural traditions and for ceremonial purposes. Countries such as the United Kingdom still use horse-drawn carriages to convey royalty and other VIPs to and from certain culturally significant events.<sup id="cite_ref-211" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-211"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Public exhibitions are another example, such as the <a href="/wiki/Budweiser_Clydesdales" title="Budweiser Clydesdales">Budweiser Clydesdales</a>, seen in parades and other public settings, a team of <a href="/wiki/Draft_horse" title="Draft horse">draft horses</a> that pull a beer wagon similar to that used before the invention of the modern motorized truck.<sup id="cite_ref-212" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-212"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses are frequently used in television, films and literature. They are sometimes featured as a major character in films about particular animals, but also used as visual elements that assure the accuracy of historical stories.<sup id="cite_ref-213" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-213"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Both live horses and <a href="/wiki/Cultural_icon" title="Cultural icon">iconic</a> images of horses are used in <a href="/wiki/Advertising" title="Advertising">advertising</a> to promote a variety of products.<sup id="cite_ref-214" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-214"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The horse frequently appears in coats of arms in <a href="/wiki/Heraldry" title="Heraldry">heraldry</a>, in a variety of poses and equipment.<sup id="cite_ref-215" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-215"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Mythology" class="mw-redirect" title="Mythology">mythologies</a> of many cultures, including <a href="/wiki/Greco-Roman_mythology" class="mw-redirect" title="Greco-Roman mythology">Greco-Roman</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hindu_mythology" title="Hindu mythology">Hindu</a>, <a href="/wiki/Islamic_mythology" title="Islamic mythology">Islamic</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Horses_in_Germanic_paganism" title="Horses in Germanic paganism">Germanic</a>, include references to both normal horses and those with wings or additional limbs, and multiple myths also call upon the horse to draw the chariots of the Moon and Sun.<sup id="cite_ref-216" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-216"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The horse also appears in the 12-year cycle of animals in the <a href="/wiki/Chinese_astrology" title="Chinese astrology">Chinese zodiac</a> related to the <a href="/wiki/Chinese_calendar" title="Chinese calendar">Chinese calendar</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-217" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-217"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses serve as the inspiration for many modern automobile names and logos, including the <a href="/wiki/Ford_Pinto" title="Ford Pinto">Ford Pinto</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ford_Bronco" title="Ford Bronco">Ford Bronco</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ford_Mustang" title="Ford Mustang">Ford Mustang</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hyundai_Equus" title="Hyundai Equus">Hyundai Equus</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hyundai_Pony" title="Hyundai Pony">Hyundai Pony</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mitsubishi_Starion" title="Mitsubishi Starion">Mitsubishi Starion</a>, <a href="/wiki/Subaru_BRAT" title="Subaru BRAT">Subaru Brumby</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mitsubishi_Colt" title="Mitsubishi Colt">Mitsubishi Colt</a>/<a href="/wiki/Dodge_Colt" title="Dodge Colt">Dodge Colt</a>, <a href="/wiki/Steyr-Puch_Pinzgauer" title="Steyr-Puch Pinzgauer">Pinzgauer</a>, <a href="/wiki/Steyr-Puch_Haflinger" title="Steyr-Puch Haflinger">Steyr-Puch Haflinger</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pegaso" title="Pegaso">Pegaso</a>, <a href="/wiki/Porsche" title="Porsche">Porsche</a>, <a href="/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Camargue" title="Rolls-Royce Camargue">Rolls-Royce Camargue</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ferrari" title="Ferrari">Ferrari</a>, <a href="/wiki/Carlsson_(car_company)" title="Carlsson (car company)">Carlsson</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kamaz" title="Kamaz">Kamaz</a>, <a href="/wiki/Corre_La_Licorne" title="Corre La Licorne">Corre La Licorne</a>, <a href="/wiki/Iran_Khodro" title="Iran Khodro">Iran Khodro</a>, <a href="/wiki/Eicher_Motors" title="Eicher Motors">Eicher</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Baojun" title="Baojun">Baojun</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-218" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-218"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-219" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-219"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-220" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-220"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Indian <a href="/wiki/TVS_Motor_Company" title="TVS Motor Company">TVS Motor Company</a> also uses a horse on their motorcycles & scooters.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Therapeutic_use">Therapeutic use</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Equine-assisted_therapy" title="Equine-assisted therapy">Equine-assisted therapy</a> and <a href="/wiki/Therapeutic_horseback_riding" class="mw-redirect" title="Therapeutic horseback riding">Therapeutic horseback riding</a></div>
<p>People of all ages with physical and mental disabilities obtain beneficial results from an association with horses. Therapeutic riding is used to mentally and physically stimulate disabled persons and help them improve their lives through improved balance and coordination, increased self-confidence, and a greater feeling of freedom and independence.<sup id="cite_ref-221" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-221"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The benefits of equestrian activity for people with disabilities has also been recognized with the addition of equestrian events to the <a href="/wiki/Paralympic_Games" title="Paralympic Games">Paralympic Games</a> and recognition of <a href="/wiki/Para-equestrian" title="Para-equestrian">para-equestrian</a> events by the <a href="/wiki/International_Federation_for_Equestrian_Sports" title="International Federation for Equestrian Sports">International Federation for Equestrian Sports</a> (FEI).<sup id="cite_ref-222" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-222"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Hippotherapy and therapeutic horseback riding are names for different physical, occupational, and speech therapy treatment strategies that use equine movement. In hippotherapy, a therapist uses the horse's movement to improve their patient's cognitive, coordination, balance, and fine motor skills, whereas therapeutic horseback riding uses specific riding skills.<sup id="cite_ref-223" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-223"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses also provide psychological benefits to people whether they actually ride or not. "Equine-assisted" or "equine-facilitated" therapy is a form of experiential <a href="/wiki/Psychotherapy" title="Psychotherapy">psychotherapy</a> that uses horses as companion animals to assist people with mental illness, including anxiety disorders, psychotic disorders, mood disorders, behavioral difficulties, and those who are going through major life changes.<sup id="cite_ref-224" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-224"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> There are also experimental programs using horses in <a href="/wiki/Prison" title="Prison">prison</a> settings. Exposure to horses appears to improve the behavior of inmates and help reduce <a href="/wiki/Recidivism" title="Recidivism">recidivism</a> when they leave.<sup id="cite_ref-225" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-225"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Products">Products</h3></div>
<p>Horses are raw material for many products made by humans throughout history, including byproducts from the <a href="/wiki/Horse_slaughter" title="Horse slaughter">slaughter</a> of horses as well as materials collected from living horses.
</p><p>Products collected from living horses include mare's milk, used by people with large horse herds, such as the <a href="/wiki/Mongols" title="Mongols">Mongols</a>, who let it ferment to produce <a href="/wiki/Kumis" title="Kumis">kumis</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-NewYorker_226-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NewYorker-226"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horse blood was once used as food by the Mongols and other <a href="/wiki/Nomad" title="Nomad">nomadic</a> tribes, who found it a convenient source of nutrition when traveling. Drinking their own horses' blood allowed the Mongols to ride for extended periods of time without stopping to eat.<sup id="cite_ref-NewYorker_226-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NewYorker-226"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The drug <a href="/wiki/Premarin" class="mw-redirect" title="Premarin">Premarin</a> is a mixture of <a href="/wiki/Estrogen" title="Estrogen">estrogens</a> extracted from the urine of pregnant mares (<b>pre</b>gnant <b>mar</b>es' ur<b>in</b>e), and was previously a widely used drug for <a href="/wiki/Hormone_replacement_therapy" title="Hormone replacement therapy">hormone replacement therapy</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-227" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-227"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The tail hair of horses can be used for making <a href="/wiki/Bow_(music)" title="Bow (music)">bows</a> for <a href="/wiki/String_instrument" title="String instrument">string instruments</a> such as the <a href="/wiki/Violin" title="Violin">violin</a>, <a href="/wiki/Viola" title="Viola">viola</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cello" title="Cello">cello</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Double_bass" title="Double bass">double bass</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-228" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-228"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Horse_meat" title="Horse meat">Horse meat</a> has been used as food for humans and <a href="/wiki/Carnivore" title="Carnivore">carnivorous animals</a> throughout the ages. Approximately 5 million horses are slaughtered each year for meat worldwide.<sup id="cite_ref-229" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-229"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> It is eaten in many parts of the world, though consumption is <a href="/wiki/Taboo" title="Taboo">taboo</a> in some cultures,<sup id="cite_ref-USDA_230-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-USDA-230"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and a subject of political controversy in others.<sup id="cite_ref-231" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-231"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horsehide leather has been used for boots, gloves, <a href="/wiki/A-2_jacket" title="A-2 jacket">jackets</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-by-product_232-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-by-product-232"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Baseball_(ball)" title="Baseball (ball)">baseballs</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-233" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-233"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and baseball gloves. Horse hooves can also be used to produce <a href="/wiki/Animal_glue" title="Animal glue">animal glue</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-234" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-234"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Horse bones can be used to make implements.<sup id="cite_ref-235" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-235"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Specifically, in Italian cuisine, the horse <a href="/wiki/Tibia" title="Tibia">tibia</a> is sharpened into a probe called a <i>spinto</i>, which is used to test the readiness of a (pig) ham as it cures.<sup id="cite_ref-236" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-236"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In Asia, the <i>saba</i> is a horsehide vessel used in the production of kumis.<sup id="cite_ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Care">Care</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Horse_care" class="mw-redirect" title="Horse care">Horse care</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Equine_nutrition" title="Equine nutrition">Equine nutrition</a>, <a href="/wiki/Horse_grooming" title="Horse grooming">Horse grooming</a>, <a href="/wiki/Veterinary_medicine" title="Veterinary medicine">Veterinary medicine</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Farrier" title="Farrier">Farrier</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Army.mil-2008-04-18-100048.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A young man in US military clothing examines the teeth of a bay (dark brown) horse, while another person in military work clothing, partially obscured, holds the horse. Several other people are partially visible in the background." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Army.mil-2008-04-18-100048.jpg/250px-Army.mil-2008-04-18-100048.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="162" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Army.mil-2008-04-18-100048.jpg/500px-Army.mil-2008-04-18-100048.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3522" data-file-height="2286"></a><figcaption>Checking teeth and other physical examinations are an important part of horse care.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Horses are grazing animals, and their major source of nutrients is good-quality forage from <a href="/wiki/Hay" title="Hay">hay</a> or <a href="/wiki/Pasture" title="Pasture">pasture</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-238" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-238"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They can consume approximately 2% to 2.5% of their body weight in dry feed each day. Therefore, a 450-kilogram (990 lb) adult horse could eat up to 11 kilograms (24 lb) of food.<sup id="cite_ref-Penn_239-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Penn-239"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Sometimes, concentrated feed such as <a href="/wiki/Cereal" title="Cereal">grain</a> is fed in addition to pasture or hay, especially when the animal is very active.<sup id="cite_ref-240" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-240"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When grain is fed, equine nutritionists recommend that 50% or more of the animal's diet by weight should still be forage.<sup id="cite_ref-Feeding_factors_241-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Feeding_factors-241"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses require a plentiful supply of clean water, a minimum of 38 to 45 litres (10 to 12 US gal) per day.<sup id="cite_ref-242" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-242"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Although horses are adapted to live outside, they require shelter from the wind and <a href="/wiki/Precipitation" title="Precipitation">precipitation</a>, which can range from a simple shed or shelter to an elaborate <a href="/wiki/Stable" title="Stable">stable</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-243" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-243"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Horses require routine <a href="/wiki/Horse_hoof" title="Horse hoof">hoof</a> care from a <a href="/wiki/Farrier" title="Farrier">farrier</a>, as well as <a href="/wiki/Vaccination" title="Vaccination">vaccinations</a> to protect against various diseases, and <a href="/wiki/Horse_teeth" title="Horse teeth">dental</a> examinations from a <a href="/wiki/Veterinarian" title="Veterinarian">veterinarian</a> or a specialized equine dentist.<sup id="cite_ref-244" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-244"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> If horses are kept inside in a barn, they require regular daily exercise for their physical health and mental well-being.<sup id="cite_ref-245" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-245"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When turned outside, they require well-maintained, sturdy <a href="/wiki/Agricultural_fencing" title="Agricultural fencing">fences</a> to be safely contained.<sup id="cite_ref-246" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-246"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Regular <a href="/wiki/Horse_grooming" title="Horse grooming">grooming</a> is also helpful to help the horse maintain good health of the hair coat and underlying skin.<sup id="cite_ref-247" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-247"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Climate_change">Climate change</h3></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1066933788">.mw-parser-output .excerpt-hat .mw-editsection-like{font-style:normal}</style><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock#Equines" title="Effects of climate change on livestock">Effects of climate change on livestock § Equines</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock&action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Kang_2023_heat_transfer_horses.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Kang_2023_heat_transfer_horses.png/250px-Kang_2023_heat_transfer_horses.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Kang_2023_heat_transfer_horses.png/500px-Kang_2023_heat_transfer_horses.png 2x" data-file-width="1499" data-file-height="1004"></a><figcaption>A diagram of heat regulation in horses<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_Kang2023_248-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_Kang2023-248"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>In 2019, there were around 17 million horses in the world. Healthy body temperature for adult horses is in the range between 37.5 and 38.5 °C (99.5 and 101.3 °F), which they can maintain while ambient temperatures are between 5 and 25 °C (41 and 77 °F). Strenuous exercise increases core body temperature by 1 °C (1.8 °F)/minute, as 80% of the energy used by equine muscles is released as heat. Along with <a href="/wiki/Bovine" class="mw-redirect" title="Bovine">bovines</a> and <a href="/wiki/Primate" title="Primate">primates</a>, equines are one of the very few animal groups which use <a href="/wiki/Sweating" class="mw-redirect" title="Sweating">sweating</a> as their primary method of thermoregulation. It can account for up to 70% of their heat loss, and horses sweat three times more than humans while undergoing comparably strenuous physical activity.<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor_249-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor-249"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Unlike in humans, this sweat is created not by <a href="/wiki/Eccrine_gland" class="mw-redirect" title="Eccrine gland">eccrine glands</a> but by <a href="/wiki/Apocrine" title="Apocrine">apocrine glands</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor_249-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor-249"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In hot conditions, horses during three hours' moderate-intensity exercise can lose 30 to 35 L of water and 100g of sodium, 198 g of choloride and 45 g of potassium.<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor_249-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McCutcheon_Geor-249"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In another difference from humans, their sweat is <a href="/wiki/Hypertonic" class="mw-redirect" title="Hypertonic">hypertonic</a>, and contains a protein called <a href="/wiki/BPIFA4P" title="BPIFA4P">latherin</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McDonald_Fleming_Beeley_Bovell_250-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_McDonald_Fleming_Beeley_Bovell-250"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which enables it to spread across their body easier, and to <a href="/wiki/Foam" title="Foam">foam</a>, rather than to drip off. 
</p><p>These adaptations are partly to compensate for their lower body surface-to-mass ratio, which makes it more difficult for horses to passively radiate heat. Prolonged exposure to very hot or humid conditions will lead to consequences such as <a href="/wiki/Anhidrosis" class="mw-redirect" title="Anhidrosis">anhidrosis</a>, <a href="/wiki/Heat_stroke" title="Heat stroke">heat stroke</a>, or brain damage, potentially culminating in death if not addressed with measures like cold water applications. Around 10% of incidents associated with horse transport have been attributed to heat stress. These issues are expected to worsen in the future.<sup id="cite_ref-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_Kang2023_248-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_on_livestock_Kang2023-248"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/African_horse_sickness" title="African horse sickness">African horse sickness</a> (AHS) is a viral illness with a mortality close to 90% in horses, and 50% in <a href="/wiki/Mule" title="Mule">mules</a>. A midge, <a href="/wiki/Culicoides_imicola" title="Culicoides imicola">Culicoides imicola</a>, is the primary vector of AHS, and its spread is expected to benefit from climate change.<sup id="cite_ref-251" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-251"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The spillover of <a href="/wiki/Hendra_virus" title="Hendra virus">Hendra virus</a> from its <a href="/wiki/Flying_fox" class="mw-redirect" title="Flying fox">flying fox</a> hosts to horses is also likely to increase, as future warming would expand the hosts' geographic range. It has been estimated that under the "moderate" and high <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_scenario" title="Climate change scenario">climate change scenarios</a>, <a href="/wiki/Representative_Concentration_Pathway" title="Representative Concentration Pathway">RCP4.5</a> and RCP8.5, the number of threatened horses would increase by 110,000 and 165,000, respectively, or by 175 and 260%.<sup id="cite_ref-252" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-252"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
</div></div>
<div style="clear:both;" class=""></div>
